proposals.title: Designing and Validating Multi-Target Probe Cocktails for SARS-CoV-2 Using Network Stability Principles
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in chemical probe and drug discovery prioritizes high selectivity for single targets, yet complex diseases like COVID-19 involve redundant viral and host pathways where single-target agents often fail. Pre-2026 work, including from the Shoichet lab, has demonstrated the power of docking to discover novel chemotypes against targets like SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro), but these efforts remain largely single-target focused. This proposal addresses the unmet need for a rational framework to design multi-target probe sets that collectively modulate disease networks more effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: A probe cocktail, designed by modeling the collective polypharmacology of its components against the SARS-CoV-2-host interaction network, will produce a more stable and resilient phenotypic suppression of viral replication in cells than a matched set of highly selective single-target probes.
proposals.method: We will computationally design probe cocktails by first defining a target network for SARS-CoV-2, including viral proteins (e.g., Mpro, PLpro) and critical host factors. Using the ZINC20 library and the DOCK3.7 algorithm, we will identify candidate compounds with characterized, multi-target docking profiles against this network. We will then adapt ecological network stability models (e.g., Lyapunov stability analysis applied to interaction matrices) to score combinations of these compounds for their ability to confer robustness to the system. Top-ranked cocktails will be synthesized or sourced and validated in antiviral assays measuring viral titer and host cell response, comparing them to single-target controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a novel, network-based design principle in chemical probe discovery, moving beyond single-target dogma. It will deliver a validated, multi-target probe set for SARS-CoV-2 research and a generalizable computational-experimental pipeline for applying ecological stability theory to pharmacological intervention.
proposals.novelty: While polypharmacology is recognized, the explicit use of ecological network stability models to rationally design and score combinations of intentionally promiscuous probes is a novel conceptual and methodological advance. This contrasts with existing combination screens, which are largely empirical.

proposals.title: Systematic Design of Polypharmacological Probe Sets for SARS-CoV-2 Antiviral Discovery via Multi-Target Docking and Experimental Validation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 antiviral discovery, particularly against SARS-CoV-2, has predominantly focused on developing highly selective inhibitors for single targets like the main protease (Mpro). However, viral replication is a multi-step process, and single-target agents are vulnerable to resistance. A polypharmacological strategy, where a compound or a set of compounds modulates multiple viral targets, could offer more robust and durable antiviral effects, but systematic methods for designing such multi-target probe sets are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: That a computational strategy, leveraging large-scale docking against multiple essential SARS-CoV-2 targets, can identify chemically tractable scaffolds that yield validated probe sets with defined polypharmacological profiles, offering superior starting points for antiviral development compared to single-target probes.
proposals.method: We will employ the DOCK3.7 suite to perform ultra-large library docking (ULLD) of 10+ million commercially available compounds against three key, structurally characterized SARS-CoV-2 targets: the main protease (Mpro), the papain-like protease (PLpro), and the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) non-structural protein 12 (nsp12). We will develop a multi-target scoring and ranking algorithm to prioritize compounds predicted to bind to two or more targets with moderate affinity. Top-ranking virtual hits will be experimentally tested in biochemical and cell-based antiviral assays for each target, followed by medicinal chemistry optimization to refine the polypharmacological profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly available, experimentally validated set of polypharmacological probes for SARS-CoV-2, along with a generalizable computational pipeline for multi-target probe design. It will provide a concrete framework for challenging the single-target dogma in early-stage antiviral discovery and generate novel chemical starting points with potentially higher barriers to resistance.
proposals.novelty: While polypharmacology is recognized, its deliberate implementation via ULLD against multiple viral targets to create defined probe sets is novel. This moves beyond retrospective analysis or phenotypic screening to a prospective, structure-based design of multi-target antivirals from the outset.

proposals.title: Ultra-Large-Scale Docking of a 100-Million-Compound Library Against SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) for the Discovery of Novel Antiviral Scaffolds
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a critical, conserved antiviral drug target. While several inhibitors exist, the discovery of novel, non-covalent chemical scaffolds with improved pharmacological properties remains a high priority for pandemic preparedness. Ultra-large-scale virtual screening, as demonstrated by the Shoichet lab for GPCRs, offers a powerful, pre-2026 method to explore vast chemical space for such leads.
proposals.hypothesis: Ultra-large-scale docking of a 100-million-molecule library against the Mpro active site will identify novel, potent, and synthetically accessible non-covalent inhibitors that are chemically distinct from existing scaffolds like nirmatrelvir.
proposals.method: We will apply the established DOCK3.7 pipeline, using the crystal structure of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro (e.g., PDB: 6LU7). A 100-million-molecule library, assembled from ZINC20 and Enamine REAL databases, will be screened. The top 50,000 ranked compounds will be clustered by chemical similarity, and the top 500 representatives will be selected for experimental purchase and biochemical testing in a fluorescence-based Mpro inhibition assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly accessible dataset of docking scores and experimental validation for thousands of compounds against Mpro. It will identify novel, experimentally confirmed hit series for further optimization, expanding the antiviral chemical arsenal. The project will also benchmark docking performance for Mpro at an unprecedented scale.
proposals.novelty: While Mpro docking screens have been performed, none have approached the scale of 100 million compounds, a method recently pioneered by this lab for other target classes. This project uniquely applies this cutting-edge, pre-2026 computational capacity to a high-impact antiviral target to systematically explore uncharted chemical space.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Grid-Based Implicit Solvation Scoring Function for Ultra-Large Library Docking Against Viral Targets
proposals.motivation: Current docking scoring functions, including those used in the Shoichet lab's ultra-large library screens against targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, often rely on vacuum or simplistic solvation models, which can limit the accuracy of binding affinity predictions. The exclusion of explicit solvation effects is a known source of error in virtual screening, leading to false positives and missed opportunities. Incorporating a more rigorous treatment of solvation directly into the grid-based energy evaluation used for high-throughput docking would address a critical bottleneck in structure-based drug discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an implicit solvation model, such as the Generalized Born (GB) or Poisson-Boltzmann (PB) model, into the pre-computed grid-based scoring framework will significantly improve the correlation between docking scores and experimental binding affinities for viral proteases and other drug targets, without sacrificing the computational speed required for screening billions of molecules.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark multiple implicit solvation models (e.g., GB/SA, PBSA) within the DOCK 3.7 framework, adapting them to work with pre-computed energy grids. The core technical challenge is the accurate and efficient approximation of solvent effects, which are typically pairwise, within a grid-based system that assumes pairwise decomposability. We will develop a hybrid approach where the solvation contribution for the protein-ligand complex is calculated on-the-fly using a fast GB model, referencing pre-computed grid maps for atomic parameters. This method will be validated by re-docking known active and decoy compounds against targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and the mu opioid receptor, comparing the new scoring function's performance to the standard DOCK score and other widely used functions like AutoDock Vina.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source scoring function that enables more accurate and physically realistic virtual screening at the scale of ultra-large libraries. It will directly enhance the lab's ability to prioritize antiviral and other therapeutic compounds for experimental testing. The resulting methodology will be disseminated as part of the DOCK software and through a public database of docking results, setting a new standard for efficiency and accuracy in computational screening.
proposals.novelty: While implicit solvation models are well-established in molecular mechanics, their efficient integration into a grid-based docking engine optimized for screening billions of compounds is non-trivial and represents a significant technical advance. This approach moves beyond simply adding a post-docking solvation correction, instead embedding solvation physics directly into the high-throughput scoring pipeline.

proposals.title: Computational Design of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) Variants with Enhanced Affinity for Non-Covalent Inhibitors Using Large-Scale Inverse Docking
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a validated drug target, but designing potent, broad-spectrum antivirals is challenging due to potential resistance mutations. The inverse problem—designing optimal receptor sequences for a given ligand—could identify protease variants with pre-emptive high affinity for existing inhibitor scaffolds, guiding the development of more resilient therapeutics and illuminating principles of molecular recognition. This addresses a critical gap, as most computational efforts focus on ligand optimization, not receptor design.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically mutating residues in the Mpro active site and scoring against a library of known non-covalent inhibitor scaffolds using ultra-large-scale inverse docking, we can computationally design protease variants with significantly enhanced predicted binding affinity, revealing design rules for robust antiviral targets.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DOCK3.8 infrastructure and the ZINC20 library of commercially available compounds. Starting from the Mpro crystal structure (e.g., 6LU7), we will define a flexible residue set within 8Å of the active site. Using a genetic algorithm, we will propose mutant sequences, generate corresponding 3D structures with RosettaCM, and perform inverse docking of a focused library of ~1 million drug-like fragments and known Mpro inhibitor cores against each variant. DOCK's grid-based scoring will enable rapid evaluation of billions of poses to identify top ligand-variant pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational pipeline for inverse protein design against small molecules and a ranked set of designed Mpro variants with predicted enhanced affinity for specific chemotypes. It will provide foundational insights into how binding sites can be tuned for pre-selected ligands, a strategy with applications in designing resilient drug targets and engineering biosensors.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking for ligand discovery is routine, applying this scale and throughput to systematically design and score receptor variants for a given ligand library is a fundamentally inverted and unexplored approach, moving beyond single-point mutagenesis to combinatorial active site redesign.

proposals.title: Dynamic, Region-Specific Grid Generation for Ultra-Large-Scale Docking Campaigns Against Viral Targets
proposals.motivation: Ultra-large library docking against targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro or viral proteases is computationally intensive, with pre-calculated receptor grids consuming significant memory and limiting scalability. As virtual libraries expand into the billions, this memory bottleneck prevents the efficient screening of diverse chemical space on accessible high-performance computing clusters. A method to generate grids on-demand for only the relevant binding region would dramatically reduce memory overhead and enable larger, more efficient screens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic grid generation system, which computes electrostatic and van der Waals potentials only for voxels proximal to a ligand's predicted pose during docking, can maintain docking accuracy while drastically reducing memory requirements compared to a static, full-receptor grid.
proposals.method: We will develop a plugin for the DOCK 3.8 or AutoDock Vina frameworks. The system will use a fast, pre-computed spatial hash map of receptor atom coordinates and charges. During docking, for each ligand pose sampled by the search algorithm, a minimal bounding box around the ligand will be defined, and the grid potentials for that sub-volume will be computed in real-time using a truncated version of the classical AMBER or CHARMM force field potentials. This will be integrated with a caching layer to store recently computed grid blocks for ligands exploring similar regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an open-source tool that enables billion-molecule docking campaigns on hardware with limited GPU or RAM resources, directly applicable to antiviral discovery pipelines. It will expand the accessible chemical space for targets of interest to the Shoichet lab, such as viral proteases and GPCRs, by removing a key technical bottleneck in large-scale virtual screening.
proposals.novelty: While grid masking exists, generating the physical-chemical potentials dynamically and on-demand during the docking search, rather than from a pre-computed full map, is a novel architectural shift. This moves beyond simply ignoring irrelevant grid points to computing them only when needed, a concept not fully implemented in mainstream docking software pre-2026.

proposals.title: Computational identification and experimental validation of catalytic residues that enhance SARS-CoV-2 Mpro stability
proposals.motivation: A central dogma in protein engineering is that functional residues, particularly those in active sites, often destabilize the protein fold due to their chemical and geometric constraints. This paradigm limits the design of robust enzymes and antiviral targets. The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a critical drug target where understanding the stability-function relationship could inform the design of more resilient inhibitors and guide resistance-proof therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of residues essential for Mpro's catalytic function also make favorable, net-positive contributions to its thermodynamic stability, and that these can be identified through large-scale computational analysis and validated experimentally.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DOCK 3.7 suite and the ZINC20 library to perform large-scale, high-throughput virtual alanine scanning of the Mpro active site and surrounding regions, quantifying predicted binding energy changes of the native substrate as a proxy for function and using Rosetta ddG_monomer for stability predictions. Machine learning (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting models trained on pre-2026 stability databases) will identify features distinguishing stabilizing functional residues. Top computational hits will be experimentally tested via site-directed mutagenesis, measuring catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM) and thermal stability (Tm) using fluorescence-based assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic map of function-stability relationships in a therapeutically vital viral protease, challenging the simplistic view that function inherently destabilizes. It will deliver a computational framework for identifying 'dual-purpose' residues, offering new strategies for designing stable enzyme variants and for targeting structurally constrained, conserved sites in antiviral drug discovery.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking and alanine scanning are established, their combined application to explicitly search for functional residues that enhance stability—rather than merely tolerate mutation—is a novel inversion of the typical stability-function trade-off analysis. Applying this to a high-profile antiviral target like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro grounds the fundamental question in a pressing biomedical context.

proposals.title: Computational Design of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Mutants to Probe Stability-Function Trade-offs in Antiviral Drug Discovery
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a validated antiviral target, but its mutational landscape and the relationship between protein stability and catalytic/drug-binding function are poorly understood. Understanding this trade-off is critical for predicting resistance mutations and designing robust inhibitors. Pre-2026 computational protein design offers a powerful, untapped approach to systematically explore this relationship in silico before experimental validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mutations predicted by computational design algorithms to stabilize the Mpro structure will be enriched for those that impair catalytic function or inhibitor binding, directly testing a stability-function trade-off.
proposals.method: We will use the RosettaDesign suite and the FoldX force field to generate and score point mutations across the Mpro structure, focusing on the active site and dimer interface. Designs will be filtered for predicted stability (ΔΔG) and ranked. Top predicted stabilizing, destabilizing, and neutral mutants will be selected for experimental testing of catalytic activity (FRET assay) and binding affinity (against known inhibitors from our docking libraries) in collaboration with virology labs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a mapped set of Mpro variants that explicitly link computational stability predictions to experimental function, providing a quantitative framework for the stability-function trade-off. It will also generate novel reagents (mutant proteins) for testing the resilience of current and future Mpro inhibitors against potential resistance.
proposals.novelty: While Shoichet's lab has extensively docked small molecules against static targets, this proposal inverts the paradigm by computationally designing the target protein itself to probe fundamental biophysical principles impacting drug discovery. Applying protein design algorithms to an antiviral target to systematically test a stability-function hypothesis is a novel synthesis of computational biophysics and virology.

proposals.title: Mapping Fitness Landscapes of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) Using Deep Mutational Scanning and High-Throughput Docking to Identify Broad-Spectrum Antiviral Scaffolds
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a conserved, essential antiviral target, but viral evolution can lead to resistance mutations that compromise drug efficacy. Pre-2026, the mutational tolerance and fitness trade-offs of Mpro are not comprehensively mapped, limiting the rational design of robust inhibitors. A systematic understanding of how mutations affect both protein stability and function is critical for developing next-generation antivirals resilient to viral escape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep mutational scanning of Mpro will reveal specific mutational trajectories that confer drug resistance while incurring fitness costs, and that high-throughput virtual screening against these mutant ensembles will identify novel chemical scaffolds with broader coverage against potential escape variants.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive Mpro mutant library using saturation mutagenesis and assess functional fitness via a yeast-based complementation assay coupled with deep sequencing. Stability changes will be quantified in parallel using a cellular thermal shift assay (CETSA) format. The resulting fitness-stability landscape data will be integrated into the ZINC22 library docking pipeline. We will dock 10+ million make-on-demand compounds against both wild-type and a curated set of high-fitness mutant Mpro structures using DOCK3.7, prioritizing hits predicted to maintain binding affinity across multiple variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution fitness-stability trade-off map for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, a public resource for the virology and drug discovery communities. It will directly yield a set of experimentally validated, broad-spectrum Mpro inhibitor chemotypes with characterized resistance profiles, advancing the goal of pandemic-prepared antivirals.
proposals.novelty: While deep mutational scanning and docking are established separately, their integrated application to map actionable drug resistance pathways for a major antiviral target is novel. The approach moves beyond static wild-type targeting to proactively design against likely evolutionary escape routes.

proposals.title: De novo design of minimal peptide-based inhibitors of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) using ultra-large library docking and functional screening
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) is a critical antiviral target, but most inhibitors are complex drug-like molecules. Identifying minimal functional motifs, such as short peptides, that can disrupt Mpro activity could reveal new, synthetically accessible chemotypes and fundamental principles of molecular recognition. This approach aligns with the need for rapid, structure-based discovery of antiviral leads against emerging coronaviruses.
proposals.hypothesis: Ultra-large library docking against the Mpro active site, focused on conformationally constrained short peptides (e.g., cyclic 5-7mers), will identify minimal sequences that retain high-affinity binding and inhibitory function, serving as both novel leads and probes of essential catalytic site interactions.
proposals.method: We will generate a virtual library of >10 million cyclic peptides using the `make_rotlib` and `bbdepo` tools within the DOCK 3.8 framework, incorporating non-canonical amino acids for diversity. This library will be docked against the high-resolution Mpro structure (PDB: 6LU7) using the Shoichet lab's established large-scale docking pipeline. Top-ranking virtual hits will be synthesized via solid-phase peptide synthesis and tested for inhibition in a fluorescence-based Mpro enzymatic assay, followed by validation of binding via surface plasmon resonance (SPR).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of minimal, peptide-based Mpro inhibitors, expanding the chemical space for coronavirus antivirals. It will also provide a methodological framework for applying ultra-large library docking to peptide and minimal scaffold discovery, bridging computational screening and peptide engineering.
proposals.novelty: While the Shoichet lab has docked millions of small molecules, applying this scale of virtual screening to designed peptide libraries for Mpro is unexplored. The focus on discovering minimal functional motifs, rather than optimizing known scaffolds, represents a distinct, first-principles approach to protease inhibitor design.

proposals.title: De Novo Design of G Protein-Biased Mu Opioid Receptor Agonists via Generative Adversarial Networks and Structure-Based Docking
proposals.motivation: The opioid crisis demands novel analgesics with reduced side effects like respiratory depression and addiction. Pre-2026 research indicates that G protein-biased agonists of the mu opioid receptor (MOR) may provide analgesia with less β-arrestin recruitment, potentially improving safety. However, discovering such biased ligands remains challenging, requiring exploration of novel chemical space beyond traditional opioid scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative machine learning models, conditioned on structural data from the MOR active site and known biased ligand pharmacophores, can produce novel, synthetically accessible molecular structures with predicted high-affinity, G protein-biased signaling profiles.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network (cWGAN) on a large library of drug-like molecules, using molecular fingerprints and 3D pharmacophore constraints derived from the published active-state MOR structure (PDB: 5C1M) and known biased ligands. The generator will be optimized to produce molecules that are both chemically valid and score highly in a parallel docking screen against the MOR using the DOCK 3.7 suite, a method deeply validated in the Shoichet lab. Top-ranking virtual hits will be synthesized and tested in vitro for binding, cAMP inhibition, and β-arrestin recruitment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new pipeline integrating generative ML with physics-based docking for target-aware molecular design. It will produce a set of novel, experimentally validated MOR ligands with characterized signaling bias, contributing chemical tools and a methodological framework for safer analgesic discovery.
proposals.novelty: While generative models for molecules existed pre-2026, their direct integration with the Shoichet lab's ultra-large-scale docking infrastructure and explicit conditioning on structural determinants of G protein bias for the MOR is a novel, synergistic approach to a critical public health problem.

proposals.title: DUD-E+: Extending a Benchmarking Database to Challenging Target Classes Including Protein-Protein Interfaces and Allosteric Sites
proposals.motivation: The Directory of Useful Decoys: Enhanced (DUD-E) has been a cornerstone for benchmarking molecular docking since 2012, but its focus on traditional orthosteric, druggable pockets does not reflect the growing interest in more challenging therapeutic targets. Pre-2026, computational drug discovery is increasingly targeting protein-protein interactions (PPIs) and allosteric sites for diseases like viral infections (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro interfaces) and GPCR-mediated disorders, yet robust, community-standard benchmarks for these target classes are lacking. This gap hinders the objective evaluation and development of docking methods tailored for these important but difficult systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by curating high-quality, experimentally validated active and decoy compound sets for protein-protein interfaces and allosteric sites, we can create a benchmark that reveals significant, previously unmeasured performance gaps in current docking methodologies, thereby guiding future algorithm development.
proposals.method: We will extend the DUD-E framework by curating new target sets from the PDB and literature up to 2025. For PPIs, we will select well-characterized interfaces with small-molecule inhibitors (e.g., MDM2-p53, IL-2). For allosteric sites, we will include targets like GPCRs (leveraging Dr. Shoichet's expertise from opioid receptor work) and kinases. We will employ the DUD-E protocol to generate property-matched decoys and use the ZINC20 library for sourcing molecules. Docking evaluations will be performed using both classical (e.g., DOCK3.7) and newer deep learning-based methods (pre-2026 implementations) to establish baseline performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with DUD-E+, a critical, publicly available resource for benchmarking docking against non-traditional targets, filling a major gap in the field. It will yield a quantitative analysis of where current docking methods succeed and fail for PPIs and allostery, directly informing future research directions in virtual screening. The database will also serve as a testbed for developing new scoring functions and sampling algorithms designed for these challenging binding sites.
proposals.novelty: While other specialized benchmarks exist, none integrate diverse, challenging target classes into the rigorous, property-matched decoy framework of DUD-E, which is the established gold standard. This proposal directly addresses the limitation of DUD-E's target scope by expanding it into the very areas that have become central to modern drug discovery, creating a unified benchmark for the next generation of docking methods.

proposals.title: AutoDUD-E: An Automated Pipeline for Continuous, Data-Driven Updates to the Directory of Useful Decoys (DUD-E) Benchmarking Set
proposals.motivation: The Directory of Useful Decoys (DUD-E) is a foundational benchmark for evaluating molecular docking methods, but its static nature (released in 2012) fails to reflect the exponential growth of public bioactivity data in resources like ChEMBL. This lag limits the assessment of modern docking algorithms against contemporary targets and chemotypes, potentially creating a performance bias towards older, well-studied systems. An automated update mechanism is critical to maintain the relevance and statistical rigor of virtual screening validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an automated pipeline can systematically curate new protein targets and ligands from public databases to produce updated, high-quality benchmarking sets that are more chemically diverse and target-representative than the original DUD-E, thereby providing a more rigorous test for current docking methodologies.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based pipeline that programmatically queries ChEMBL (via its REST API) for new protein targets with sufficient bioactivity data. For each target, we will apply DUD-E's physicochemical matching rules (using RDKit) to generate property-matched decoys from the ZINC database. The pipeline will incorporate automated structure curation (e.g., canonicalization, salt stripping) and filtering based on pre-2026 best practices for assay confidence and compound drug-likeness. A key technical challenge will be the automated selection of high-quality, non-redundant active sets and the validation of decoy sets using historical DUD-E metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a continuously updatable, open-source framework for benchmarking set generation, ensuring evaluations keep pace with chemical and biological discovery. It will yield an initial, significantly expanded 'DUD-E+2025' benchmark, enabling a critical re-assessment of docking performance on newer target classes (e.g., viral proteases, GPCRs) and chemotypes not present in the original set.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created static benchmark successors (e.g., DEKOIS 2.0, MUV), no pre-2026 system automates the *continuous* integration of new public data into the DUD-E framework. This pipeline moves beyond one-off extensions to create a living, versioned benchmark.

proposals.title: A Git-Inspired Informatics Platform for Collaborative, Version-Controlled Mapping of HCV NS5B Polymerase Inhibitor Binding Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) remains a global health burden, and its RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (NS5B) is a validated but challenging drug target due to its multiple inhibitor binding sites and rapid emergence of resistance. Current discovery efforts are fragmented, with data from different labs on diverse chemical series stored in disparate formats, hindering the systematic analysis of cross-site interactions and resistance evolution. A unified, dynamic framework is needed to integrate and version-control this complex landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: That treating individual experimental profiles (e.g., Ki, resistance mutations, co-crystal structures) as version-controlled commits within a collaborative, Git-like informatics system will enable the non-linear mapping of the NS5B inhibitor landscape, revealing unexpected correlations between distinct binding sites and accelerating the prediction of robust, cooperative inhibitor combinations.
proposals.method: We will develop a database and web interface where each experimental 'profile' for an HCV NS5B inhibitor (docking pose from the ZINC library, measured Ki, resistance mutation map, functional assay result) is stored as a unique commit with metadata. Researchers can branch (fork) from any commit to propose new analogs or hypotheses. The core will use a graph database (Neo4j) to link commits by chemical similarity, binding site, and mutational data. Docking workflows, building on the group's expertise with DOCK3.7 and large-scale virtual screening against targets like Mpro and GPCRs, will be integrated to generate new virtual compound branches for experimental testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first collaborative, version-controlled platform for mapping a complete antiviral target landscape, moving beyond static databases. It will generate a publicly available, dynamically growing map of HCV NS5B inhibitor structure-activity-resistance relationships, directly enabling the design of combination therapies that preempt resistance. The informatics framework itself will be a generalizable tool for collaborative drug discovery on other targets.
proposals.novelty: Applying distributed version control concepts from software engineering (Git) to collaboratively map the complex, evolving biochemical landscape of a viral drug target is a novel paradigm shift. It moves from linear project histories to a non-linear, branching exploration of chemical and mutational space, explicitly designed for merging disparate data streams to find synergistic insights.

proposals.title: Computational Discovery of Host-Factor Synthetic Lethality Partners for HCV-Selective Antivirals
proposals.motivation: Despite direct-acting antivirals (DAAs), HCV remains a global health burden with challenges of resistance, cost, and access. Targeting host factors offers a high barrier to resistance but risks on-target toxicity. The synthetic lethality (SL) paradigm, proven in oncology, provides a framework to identify host targets selectively essential in infected cells, potentially yielding safer host-targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HCV infection creates specific, druggable synthetic lethal vulnerabilities in human hepatocytes by co-opting host factors, and that small molecules targeting the complementary partners of these HCV-dependent factors will selectively impair infected cell viability.
proposals.method: We will first curate a network of HCV host-dependency factors from literature (e.g., miR-122, cyclophilins) and host-pathogen interaction databases. Using protein-protein interaction and pathway data (e.g., STRING, KEGG), we will computationally predict their synthetic lethal partners via guilt-by-association and mutual exclusivity analyses adapted from cancer genomics. We will then perform structure-based virtual screening of ultra-large libraries (e.g., ZINC20, Enamine REAL) against the predicted partner proteins using the DOCK3.7 suite, leveraging our lab's expertise in large-scale docking against GPCRs and viral proteases. Top-ranking compounds will be prioritized for experimental validation in HCV-infected hepatocyte models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for antiviral target discovery based on synthetic lethality and a prioritized set of candidate compounds for HCV-selective host-targeted therapy. It will establish a proof-of-concept for applying cancer biology principles to infectious disease, potentially identifying new chemical starting points with a high barrier to viral resistance.
proposals.novelty: While host-targeting for HCV is known, the systematic application of the synthetic lethality framework from oncology to define and drug complementary host partners is novel. The approach inverts the typical screening logic from the viral or primary host factor to its predicted synthetic lethal counterpart.

proposals.title: Structure-Based Virtual Screening for Host-Targeted Antivirals: Discovery of Small-Molecule Inhibitors of Human Host Factors Essential for Hepatitis C Virus Replication
proposals.motivation: Despite direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) for Hepatitis C virus (HCV), challenges remain, including cost, resistance, and limited efficacy in co-infections. Targeting host factors essential for viral replication offers a complementary, high barrier-to-resistance strategy. Pre-2026 genetic and proteomic studies have identified several host dependency factors (e.g., cyclophilin A, miR-122, and various kinases), but few have been successfully targeted by small molecules in a clinical antiviral context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structure-based virtual screening against crystallographically characterized human host proteins, which are genetically validated as essential for HCV replication, will yield novel, potent, and selective small-molecule inhibitors with antiviral activity.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale molecular docking using the DOCK3.7 suite against prepared structures of human host targets (e.g., cyclophilin A, PI4KIIIα) from the Protein Data Bank. A focused library of ~10 million commercially available, drug-like compounds will be screened, leveraging the ZINC20 database and the Shoichet lab's established docking infrastructure and experimental validation pipeline. Top-ranking virtual hits will be prioritized using interaction analysis and chemical clustering, then procured and tested in biochemical assays (e.g., cyclophilin inhibition) followed by cell-based HCV replicon and cytotoxicity assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel chemical probes that validate host targets for HCV therapy and provide a roadmap for host-targeted antiviral discovery. It will expand the application of the lab's high-throughput docking methods from viral targets (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro) and GPCRs to the challenging space of host-pathogen protein-protein interactions.
proposals.novelty: While the lab has extensive experience docking against viral and human disease targets, applying this pipeline specifically to discover antivirals via host factor inhibition inverts the conventional screening paradigm for HCV. The novelty lies in the systematic, structure-based targeting of human proteins with known viral dependency, rather than the viral proteome itself.

proposals.title: De Novo Discovery of Allosteric SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Inhibitors via Fragment Docking and Linking
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a validated antiviral target, but most clinical candidates target the active site, risking resistance. The discovery of a druggable allosteric site (e.g., the 'dimer interface' site) offers a promising orthogonal strategy. However, known allosteric chemotypes remain limited, creating a need for novel chemical matter that exploits this site.
proposals.hypothesis: Fragment-based docking and computational linking, applied to a large virtual fragment library, can generate novel, high-affinity chemical scaffolds that bind the Mpro allosteric site and inhibit its function.
proposals.method: We will dock 500,000+ commercially available fragments (e.g., from ZINC20) into the allosteric site of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro (PDB: 7L11) using the DOCK3.7 algorithm, as previously applied to GPCRs. Top-ranking fragments will be clustered and analyzed for favorable interaction patterns. Pairs of fragments occupying proximal sub-pockets will be computationally linked using the program 'BREED' and elaborated via structure-based optimization with the 'Make-It' or 'ReCore' modules in OpenEye toolkits. The resulting linked compounds will be re-docked and prioritized for synthesis and biochemical testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel, synthetically accessible chemical scaffolds targeting the Mpro allosteric site, expanding the antiviral toolkit against SARS-CoV-2. It will also provide a validated computational pipeline for fragment-based discovery against viral allosteric sites, applicable to future pandemic preparedness.
proposals.novelty: While the Shoichet lab has pioneered large-scale docking, this project uniquely applies fragment-based docking and linking specifically to a viral allosteric site for de novo inhibitor design, a strategy not yet extensively reported for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro as of 2025.

proposals.title: A Dual-Strategy, TPP-Guided Computational Library Design for Antiviral Lead Optimization Against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro
proposals.motivation: Lead optimization in antiviral drug discovery, such as for SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro), often focuses narrowly on potency, risking convergence on series with latent liabilities like poor ADMET or synthetic complexity. This can lead to late-stage attrition. A systematic, parallel strategy that balances local optimization with scaffold de-risking from the outset is needed to efficiently identify robust clinical candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational pipeline that designs two complementary libraries—one for focused SAR and one for scaffold hopping—guided by a formal, multi-parameter Target Product Profile (TPP) will generate leads with superior overall profiles compared to traditional sequential optimization.
proposals.method: We will apply this to an existing Mpro hit series. First, we will formalize a TPP with specific thresholds for potency (IC50 < 100 nM), selectivity, permeability (Caco-2 Papp), and metabolic stability (human microsomes). For the focused library, we will use analog-by-catalog searching and virtual synthesis with RDKit, followed by docking using FRED or DOCK3.8 against the Mpro structure (e.g., PDB 6LU7). For the scaffold-hopping library, we will use the DeepScaffold framework or the BREED algorithm to generate novel cores, filter for synthetic tractability using the AiZynthFinder retrosynthesis tool, and dock the resulting enumerated molecules. Both libraries will be prioritized by a TPP-weighted scoring function.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable computational framework for lead optimization that explicitly de-risks projects by exploring divergent chemical space early. It will also yield new, synthetically accessible chemotypes with promising predicted profiles against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro for experimental validation.
proposals.novelty: While scaffold hopping and TPPs are known, their integration into a single, parallelized design process that directly pits local optimization against global exploration in a quantitatively guided manner is novel. This moves beyond typical sequential workflows and adversarial library design concepts used in other target classes.

proposals.title: A Community-Driven, Iterative Platform for the Accelerated Optimization of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: The rapid discovery of antiviral chemical probes, such as inhibitors of the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro), is critical for pandemic preparedness. However, the optimization of initial hits into potent, selective probes is a slow, iterative bottleneck. Existing public databases like ZINC and the Shoichet Lab's docking results repository provide vast starting points, but lack a structured mechanism for community-driven evolution of focused libraries based on real-time experimental feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating structure-based virtual screening with a public, wiki-style platform for community annotation of experimental results will create a virtuous feedback loop that significantly accelerates the identification of structure-activity relationships (SAR) and the optimization of lead compounds against Mpro.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DOCK 3.7 algorithm and the ultra-large library docking infrastructure from the Shoichet Lab to generate an initial focused library of ~1,000 diverse Mpro inhibitors from public chemical libraries. Each synthesized and tested compound will be documented on a public MediaWiki instance, with standardized fields for structures, assay data (e.g., FRET-based Mpro inhibition), and cytotoxicity. Registered experts will annotate entries to propose SAR, flag metabolic liabilities, and suggest new analogs. These community insights will directly seed the next round of focused library design using similarity searching and matched molecular pair analysis, creating a closed-loop, iterative optimization cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly accessible platform for collaborative chemical probe discovery and a set of evolved, well-characterized Mpro inhibitors with rich, community-curated SAR data. It will demonstrate a scalable model for accelerating hit-to-lead optimization by harnessing collective intelligence, moving beyond static databases to a dynamic, data-driven design process.
proposals.novelty: While public compound databases and collaborative wikis exist separately, their tight integration into a real-time, experimental feedback loop for focused library evolution is novel. This approach transforms the community from passive data consumers into active collaborators guiding each design iteration.

proposals.title: De Novo Design of Allosteric Inhibitors for SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Using Generative Deep Learning and Structure-Based Optimization
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a validated antiviral target, but most discovery efforts focus on screening known compound libraries against its orthosteric site, limiting chemical novelty and potential for overcoming resistance. De novo design, which generates novel molecular structures optimized for a specific binding site, offers a path to unprecedented chemotypes, particularly for less-explored allosteric sites. This directly addresses a key challenge in antiviral discovery by moving beyond the constraints of existing chemical databases.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative deep learning models, conditioned on the 3D structure of the Mpro allosteric site, can produce novel, synthetically accessible small molecules that exhibit potent and selective inhibition in biochemical and cellular assays.
proposals.method: We will train a 3D conditional generative adversarial network (3D-cGAN) or a graph-based variational autoencoder (VAE) on protein-ligand complex structures from the PDB, using the Mpro allosteric site (e.g., from PDB ID 7KXG) as the conditioning input. Generated molecules will be filtered for synthetic accessibility (SAscore) and drug-likeness, then optimized via iterative cycles of docking (using the validated DOCK3.7 platform from the Shoichet lab) and scoring with the allosteric site. Top computational hits will be synthesized and tested in Mpro enzymatic and antiviral cell-based assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a set of novel, de novo designed chemical probes targeting the allosteric site of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, providing new tools to study viral protease function and resistance. It will establish a validated computational pipeline for structure-based generative chemistry, extending the lab's expertise in large-scale docking to the more challenging inverse problem of molecular design.
proposals.novelty: While the Shoichet lab has pioneered ultra-large library docking, applying generative models specifically for de novo design of allosteric inhibitors of a viral target represents a significant methodological shift from screening to creation. The integration of a robust, physics-based docking step to refine generative model output is a distinct, hybrid approach not commonly employed in early generative chemistry work.

proposals.title: Pareto Frontier Analysis for Multi-Objective Optimization in Virtual Screening: Balancing Potency and Synthesizability for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: Virtual screening of ultra-large libraries, as pioneered by the Shoichet lab, identifies potent binders but often yields hits with poor synthetic accessibility, hindering experimental validation and lead optimization. This creates a critical bottleneck in drug discovery, particularly for antiviral targets like SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro), where rapid progression to synthesis is essential. A systematic, transparent method is needed to navigate the inherent trade-off between predicted biological activity and practical synthetic feasibility early in the screening pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Pareto frontier analysis to the outputs of molecular docking will identify a subset of non-dominated compounds that optimally balance predicted binding affinity against Mpro with high synthetic accessibility scores, yielding a more actionable and chemically tractable portfolio of lead candidates than ranking by docking score alone.
proposals.method: We will dock a 10+ million compound library against the SARS-CoV-2 Mpro crystal structure (PDB: 6LU7) using DOCK3.7. For all top-scoring compounds (e.g., top 0.1%), we will compute a synthetic accessibility (SA) score using the Synthetic Accessibility (SA) Score or a similar pre-2026 method like the Ertl & Schuffenhauer approach. A Pareto frontier will be constructed from the 2D objective space of docking score (ΔG) vs. SA score using the non-dominated sorting algorithm. The resulting frontier compounds will be analyzed for chemical diversity and compared to the top-ranked compounds from docking-only screening.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for multi-objective decision-making in virtual screening, directly translating an engineering optimization paradigm to drug discovery. It will deliver a publicly accessible dataset of Pareto-optimal Mpro candidates, explicitly mapping the trade-off landscape to guide medicinal chemists. The method will be integrated into the lab's large-scale docking database for broader community use.
proposals.novelty: While multi-parameter optimization is discussed in medicinal chemistry, the explicit, algorithmic application of Pareto frontier analysis from engineering to the primary output of ultra-large library docking is novel. This moves beyond simple filtering or weighted-sum scoring to provide a complete map of optimal trade-offs.

proposals.title: Expanding the Druggable Chemical Space: Ultra-Large Library Docking of Synthetically Complex Scaffolds Against Viral Targets
proposals.motivation: Ultra-large library docking (ULLD) has proven successful in discovering novel chemotypes for targets like GPCRs and viral proteases, but current virtual libraries are heavily biased toward synthetically accessible, make-on-demand compounds. This constraint artificially limits exploration of vast regions of chemical space containing structurally novel scaffolds with potentially superior potency, selectivity, or resistance profiles, particularly for rapidly evolving antiviral targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing and docking ultra-large virtual libraries enriched with synthetically challenging but highly novel scaffolds, we will identify potent, structurally unprecedented inhibitors of viral targets that would be missed by conventional make-on-demand library screens.
proposals.method: We will generate a 10+ billion molecule virtual library by applying the REAL Space and Enamine REAL building block methodologies, but deliberately incorporating high-complexity, stereochemically rich, and macrocyclic scaffolds with known but challenging synthetic routes (e.g., via ring-closing metathesis, complex cross-couplings). This library will be docked against the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) using the validated DOCK3.7/ZINC infrastructure. Top-ranking, novel chemotypes will be prioritized using machine learning models (e.g., random forest) trained on docking scores and synthetic feasibility scores, followed by rigorous synthesis and biochemical validation for a select subset.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly accessible database of ultra-large, synthetically expanded virtual libraries and a set of experimentally validated, novel chemotypes for Mpro. It will establish a methodological framework for balancing novelty and synthetic tractability in virtual screening, pushing the boundaries of computational hit discovery.
proposals.novelty: While previous ULLD campaigns (e.g., against opioid receptors) prioritized readily synthesizable compounds, this proposal systematically relaxes the synthesis-first constraint to explicitly target high-complexity chemical space, using docking as the primary filter for novel bioactivity rather than synthetic ease.

proposals.title: Structure-Based Optimization of Novel SARS-CoV-2 Mpro Inhibitors for In Vivo Antiviral Efficacy
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by SARS-CoV-2, highlighted the urgent need for broad-spectrum oral antivirals. The main protease (Mpro) is a highly conserved and validated drug target. While high-throughput docking campaigns, including our own, have identified novel, potent Mpro inhibitors in vitro, these hits often possess suboptimal pharmacokinetic (PK) properties, limiting their translation to in vivo models and clinical use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iterative cycles of structure-based design, guided by computational predictions of ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, Toxicity) properties and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, can systematically improve the metabolic stability and oral bioavailability of our novel Mpro inhibitor scaffolds without compromising their antiviral potency.
proposals.method: We will initiate the project with our most promising, potent Mpro inhibitor chemotypes identified from the ZINC20 library docking. Using the FEP+ (Free Energy Perturbation) module in Schrödinger and the Rosetta Flex ddG protocol, we will predict binding affinity changes for focused libraries of analogs designed to improve key ADMET parameters (e.g., reducing CYP450 inhibition, improving solubility). We will employ QikProp and ADMET Predictor for property forecasting. Synthesized analogs will be tested in parallel for Mpro enzymatic inhibition, antiviral activity in cell culture, and microsomal stability. The highest-performing compounds will advance to murine PK studies and a proof-of-concept efficacy model in K18-hACE2 transgenic mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1-2 optimized Mpro inhibitor leads with demonstrated in vivo antiviral efficacy and favorable PK profiles, providing advanced candidates for further development. It will also establish a validated, integrated computational/experimental pipeline for accelerating the hit-to-lead optimization of antiviral compounds discovered via virtual screening.
proposals.novelty: While many groups have discovered Mpro hits, this proposal uniquely applies state-of-the-art free energy calculations and property-focused design specifically to novel, non-covalent chemotypes emerging from ultra-large library docking, moving them beyond in vitro potency into the realm of in vivo-viable drug candidates.

proposals.title: Generative Adversarial Networks for Synthesizable Antiviral Design: Augmenting Reaction-Based Libraries for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro
proposals.motivation: Reaction-based libraries, while synthesizable, explore a limited region of chemical space. For targets like SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro), a critical antiviral target, this can constrain the discovery of novel chemotypes. Generative models offer a powerful, complementary approach to design new, synthetically accessible molecules, but their integration with established docking workflows and reaction-based constraints remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative adversarial network (GAN) conditioned on synthetic feasibility and Mpro docking scores can design novel, synthesizable compounds that are chemically distinct from, yet complementary to, a traditional reaction-based library, yielding new antiviral leads.
proposals.method: We will train a Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP) on a large corpus of drug-like molecules. The generator will be conditioned using a recurrent neural network (RNN) to process SMILES strings. A synthetic accessibility (SA) score predictor, trained on known reactions, will be incorporated into the adversarial loss. Generated molecules will be filtered for SA and docked against the Mpro crystal structure (e.g., 6LU7) using the DOCK 3.7 algorithm, with top-scoring compounds used to fine-tune the generator via reinforcement learning, creating a closed-loop design cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available library of generative, synthesizable compounds targeting Mpro. It will demonstrate a practical framework for integrating generative AI with physics-based docking and synthetic constraints, moving beyond mere novelty to deliver actionable chemical matter. The resulting compounds will be experimentally tested, potentially yielding new antiviral scaffolds.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been used for molecule generation, their direct integration with the Shoichet lab's ultra-large-scale docking infrastructure and explicit conditioning on a reaction-based synthetic accessibility metric to complement, not replace, existing library design is a novel synthesis of methods.

proposals.title: Ultra-Large Library Docking for Target Deconvolution: A Computational Platform for Identifying Potential Protein Targets of Novel Bioactive Compounds
proposals.motivation: The discovery of novel bioactive compounds, such as antivirals, is often followed by a costly and time-consuming process of target identification (deconvolution). Pre-2026, computational methods for this inverse problem are limited in scale and accuracy. Leveraging the Shoichet lab's expertise in ultra-large library docking (e.g., docking 14 million compounds) and established databases of docked molecules, we propose a systematic, large-scale computational approach to predict potential targets for compounds of unknown mechanism, accelerating mechanistic studies and drug discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: That a compound's target can be predicted by screening it against a structurally diverse, pre-computed ultra-large library of protein binding sites, where a high-ranking docking pose against a specific target indicates a plausible mechanism of action.
proposals.method: We will build upon the ZINC database infrastructure and the DOCK 3.7 algorithm. First, we will curate a structurally diverse library of 500+ high-resolution protein structures, focusing on viral (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, other proteases) and human drug targets (e.g., GPCRs, kinases). For each target, we will use the pre-existing database of 100+ million pre-docked conformers from the ZINC library to establish baseline docking score distributions. A novel compound will then be docked against this entire target panel using the same protocol. Statistical significance (Z-score) of the compound's score against each target, relative to the background distribution, will be computed to generate a ranked list of predicted targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly accessible computational platform for target hypothesis generation. It will provide a rigorous, structure-based method to triage experimental efforts in target identification. The study will also yield a benchmark dataset of compounds with known targets to validate the approach and establish performance metrics for computational deconvolution.
proposals.novelty: While forward docking (screening compounds against a single target) is routine, inverting this process to screen one compound against a vast, pre-computed landscape of diverse targets at this scale is novel. The key innovation is using the statistical framework of pre-computed background distributions from ultra-large libraries to assess the significance of docking scores for deconvolution.

proposals.title: Pocket-Agnostic Docking for Antiviral Discovery: A Geometric Deep Learning and Consensus Scoring Approach
proposals.motivation: Structure-based virtual screening relies on defining a precise binding pocket, which is a major limitation for targets with poorly characterized or cryptic allosteric sites, or for rapidly emerging viral proteases like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro where alternative inhibition sites may exist. Pre-2026 docking databases and large-scale screens, such as those conducted by the Shoichet lab, are constrained by this requirement, potentially missing novel chemotypes that bind outside canonical sites. Developing methods that relax this prerequisite would significantly expand the scope of docking campaigns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating geometric deep learning for whole-protein surface featurization with consensus scoring from multiple, rapidly generated coarse-grained pocket hypotheses can identify high-affinity ligands without prior knowledge of a single, well-defined binding site.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will use a graph neural network (GNN) trained on protein surfaces to predict interaction hotspots and generate an ensemble of potential binding pockets using algorithms like P2Rank or Fpocket. Second, we will perform docking of ultra-large libraries (e.g., the 14M-compound ZINC library) into this ensemble of pockets using DOCK3.7 and score compounds using a consensus metric that prioritizes molecules with favorable poses across multiple pocket hypotheses. The approach will be validated on SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and other viral targets with known allosteric inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, publicly available computational pipeline for pocket-agnostic virtual screening, directly expanding the utility of large-scale docking databases. It will deliver a set of experimentally testable candidate compounds predicted to bind novel sites on high-priority antiviral targets, moving the field beyond canonical active-site discovery.
proposals.novelty: While pocket prediction and docking exist separately, their tight integration into a single screening workflow that uses consensus scoring across an ensemble of predicted sites to prioritize library compounds is novel. This moves beyond simply identifying a single alternative pocket to systematically evaluating ligandability across the entire protein surface in a single screen.

proposals.title: Systematic Validation of a Large-Scale Docking and Experimental Database Across Diverse Protein Target Classes
proposals.motivation: The Shoichet lab's recent development of a large-scale docking and experimental results database (DOCK 3.8) has been validated on a limited set of targets, primarily viral proteases (Mpro) and opioid receptors. To establish this resource as a general-purpose tool for structure-based drug discovery, its predictive power must be assessed across the full spectrum of pharmaceutically relevant protein classes, such as GPCRs, ion channels, and kinases, which have distinct binding site geometries and physicochemical properties. A broad validation is critical for the community to confidently apply this methodology to novel targets.
proposals.hypothesis: The physics-based scoring functions and high-throughput docking protocols optimized for initial targets will maintain robust enrichment and pose prediction accuracy when applied to diverse target classes, including GPCRs and ion channels, enabling the discovery of novel chemotypes.
proposals.method: We will select 10-12 high-resolution crystal structures representing GPCRs (e.g., β2-adrenergic receptor), ion channels (e.g, TRPV1, Nav1.7), kinases, and nuclear receptors from the PDB. Against each, we will dock the 14-million compound virtual library used in prior work, employing DOCK 3.8 with its AMBER-based scoring. For each target, we will computationally benchmark performance using known active/decoy sets from databases like DUD-E. Top-ranked compounds for 3-4 targets will be procured from commercial sources and tested in orthogonal biochemical and/or cellular assays to determine experimental hit rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, experimentally grounded performance assessment of a unified large-scale docking pipeline across major drug target families. It will generate a publicly accessible benchmark dataset and identify target-class-specific parameters or limitations, guiding future method development. The project may also yield novel starting points for drug discovery programs against the selected targets.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking screens are common, systematic experimental validation of a single, consistent methodology across radically different protein folds and binding sites has not been performed. This moves beyond target-specific case studies to establish general principles for virtual screening.

proposals.title: Optimizing Early Enrichment in Ultra-Large Virtual Screens: A Multi-Armed Bandit Framework for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro Inhibitor Discovery
proposals.motivation: Ultra-large virtual screens of billions of molecules, as pioneered by the Shoichet lab, present a massive computational challenge: how to intelligently select a tractable subset of ligands for experimental testing to maximize the discovery of potent hits early in the campaign. Traditional sequential docking and ranking can be inefficient, wasting resources on chemically similar regions of chemical space before identifying the most promising scaffolds. Framing this as an exploration-exploitation problem directly addresses the practical need for cost-effective and accelerated drug discovery, particularly for antiviral targets like SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a ultra-large library screen as a multi-armed bandit problem will enable more efficient ligand selection strategies than static ranking, leading to significantly higher early enrichment of experimentally confirmed hits against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment using the DOCK 3.8 platform and the Enamine REAL database. Ligand clusters, defined by topological fingerprints and scaffold, will act as 'arms.' A contextual bandit algorithm (e.g., LinUCB or a neural network-based policy) will iteratively select clusters for docking based on prior reward (docking score) and molecular context (fingerprint). The reward function will combine docking score and novelty. We will benchmark against standard sequential docking using retrospective validation with known Mpro inhibitors and prospective testing of a ~1 million compound subset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, adaptive algorithmic framework for navigating ultra-large chemical spaces, directly increasing the cost-effectiveness of virtual screening campaigns. It will yield a publicly available software tool and a prospective, experimentally validated set of Mpro inhibitors, contributing to antiviral discovery. The study will establish a principled machine learning approach to the exploration-exploitation trade-off in computational chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While bandit algorithms are used in drug design for A/B testing, their application to guide the *docking process itself* within a single, massive screen is novel. This moves beyond post-docking analysis to active, in-screening decision-making, a non-obvious synergy of reinforcement learning and physics-based docking.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Decoy Binding Pockets for the SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Using Negative Design Principles
proposals.motivation: Virtual screening campaigns against targets like the SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) often yield high hit rates but low selectivity, identifying compounds that bind promiscuously or to irrelevant sites. This leads to wasted experimental resources and obscures true actives. Pre-2026, computational protein design has successfully used negative design to avoid unwanted interactions, but this principle has not been systematically applied to the inverse problem of designing optimal decoy pockets for small-molecule docking.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly designing and incorporating computational decoy binding pockets that mimic the physicochemical properties of the target active site, we can train docking scoring functions to penalize compounds that bind promiscuously, thereby increasing the selectivity and true positive rate of virtual screens.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RosettaDesign framework to generate an ensemble of decoy pockets. Starting from the Mpro crystal structure (PDB: 6LU7), we will use the Rosetta 'fastdesign' protocol to mutate residues lining the binding pocket while preserving the backbone, aiming to create geometrically similar but chemically diverse cavities. These decoys will be integrated into the DOCK 3.8 pipeline. During screening of ultra-large libraries (e.g., the 14M-compound ZINC library), compounds will be docked against both the true Mpro pocket and the decoy ensemble; a selectivity score penalizing strong decoy binding will be calculated and used to re-rank the virtual hits.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, generalizable method for incorporating negative design into structure-based virtual screening, directly addressing the promiscuity problem. It will yield a new, selectivity-enriched ranked list of Mpro inhibitors for experimental testing and a publicly available database of decoy pockets and associated docking scores.
proposals.novelty: While decoy molecules are common in ligand-based screening, the computational design of decoy *protein pockets* as negative design elements for docking scoring is a novel concept. It inverts the typical protein design goal, applying it to improve small-molecule discovery rather than protein stability or function.

proposals.title: Target-Class-Informed Decoy Selection: A Data-Driven Strategy to Improve Virtual Screening Enrichment for Kinases and GPCRs
proposals.motivation: Large-scale docking campaigns, such as those conducted by the Shoichet lab against viral targets and GPCRs, rely on property-matched decoy libraries to benchmark performance. However, generic decoy sets like the DUD-E may not adequately capture the specific chemical and topological constraints of distinct protein families, potentially biasing enrichment metrics and obscuring true ligand discovery potential. Developing target-class-specific decoy strategies is crucial for more accurate virtual screening validation and for guiding the selection of optimal compound libraries for specific target classes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing decoy libraries informed by the binding-site chemistry and known ligand pharmacophores of specific protein families (e.g., kinases, GPCRs) will yield more realistic and challenging benchmarks, leading to improved enrichment in virtual screening and better prediction of true actives.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 database and the DOCK3.7 algorithm. For kinases and GPCRs, we will curate known actives from ChEMBL and define target-class-specific physicochemical and shape-based property spaces. We will then generate matched decoys using a multi-objective optimization that enforces property matching while also incorporating negative design elements, such as the exclusion of key pharmacophore features common to the active set (e.g., hinge-binding motifs for kinases). Performance will be validated by retrospective docking against high-resolution crystal structures of representative targets from each class, comparing enrichment (ROC-AUC, logAUC) against standard DUD-E decoys.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce publicly available, target-class-optimized decoy libraries for kinases and GPCRs, directly usable by the community. It will provide a methodological framework for creating family-specific decoy sets, leading to more reliable virtual screening benchmarks. The results will inform the design of focused screening libraries, increasing the efficiency of large-scale docking campaigns for these therapeutically critical target classes.
proposals.novelty: While property-matched decoys are standard, the strategy of intentionally 'pharmacophore-disfavoring' decoy generation tailored to a protein family's specific ligand chemistry is novel. This moves beyond generic property matching to incorporate structural knowledge directly into the decoy selection process.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Computationally Defined Decoy Sites Using Non-Binders and Promiscuous Ligands
proposals.motivation: Computational docking campaigns against targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro or GPCRs generate vast numbers of predicted actives, but many are false positives that bind to 'decoy sites'—alternative, non-physiological pockets. Pre-2026, decoy sites are defined computationally (e.g., through molecular dynamics or pocket detection algorithms), but these definitions lack rigorous experimental validation. This gap limits our ability to improve docking scoring functions and library design by systematically excluding these misleading interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that known non-binders and promiscuous, low-affinity binders will selectively bind to computationally predicted decoy sites, providing experimental evidence to validate and refine the computational definition of these sites.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DOCK 3.7 infrastructure and the ZINC20 library to perform large-scale docking against a primary target (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro) and a panel of structurally similar 'decoy' proteins. Known non-binders (from prior high-throughput screens) and promiscuous fragments will be docked. Top-ranked poses in decoy sites will be tested experimentally using crystallography and surface plasmon resonance (SPR). The computational decoy site definition will be refined using the experimental data, iteratively updating the pocket detection parameters in the FTMap or Fpocket algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental dataset directly linking computational decoy site predictions to physical ligand binding. It will create a validated benchmark for improving scoring function specificity in docking, as seen in the Shoichet lab's work on opioid receptors. The methodology will be integrated into the lab's public docking results database to guide future virtual screening campaigns away from decoy site artifacts.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard paradigm: instead of using actives to find true sites, we use non-binders and promiscuous compounds as probes to definitively identify and characterize false-positive binding sites, turning a nuisance into a diagnostic tool.

proposals.title: Integrating High-Throughput Docking with Orthogonal Biophysical Assays to Deconvolute Direct from Indirect Inhibitors of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease
proposals.motivation: High-throughput virtual screening and experimental assays often identify compounds that inhibit a target protein's activity, but these hits can act through indirect mechanisms (e.g., aggregation, assay interference, or targeting a co-factor) rather than direct binding. This is a critical problem in drug discovery, leading to wasted resources on false positives. For high-value targets like SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro), distinguishing direct binders is essential for developing robust antiviral leads.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating ultra-large library docking with orthogonal, direct biophysical assays, we can systematically classify Mpro inhibitors as direct binders or indirect modulators, revealing a more reliable chemical starting point for antiviral development.
proposals.method: We will dock 10+ million commercially available molecules from the ZINC20 library against the Mpro active site using the DOCK3.7 algorithm, as previously applied to GPCRs. Top-ranking virtual hits will be experimentally tested in a primary enzymatic inhibition assay. All active compounds will then be subjected to two orthogonal direct-binding assays: differential scanning fluorimetry (DSF) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR). Compounds confirmed in both orthogonal assays will be prioritized for crystallography or cryo-EM to validate binding poses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible dataset mapping Mpro inhibitors to their mechanism of action (direct vs. indirect), serving as a benchmark for future virtual screening campaigns. It will also deliver a set of high-confidence, chemically diverse direct binders for further optimization as SARS-CoV-2 antivirals. The integrated pipeline will be a generalizable framework for target validation in early drug discovery.
proposals.novelty: While docking and activity screens are common, the systematic triage of ultra-large library docking hits through a direct-binding assay cascade to deconvolute mechanism is not standard practice. Applying this rigorous pipeline specifically to the well-studied Mpro target will provide unprecedented clarity on the true hit rate and chemical matter for direct inhibition.

proposals.title: Synergistic Antiviral Discovery: Computational Docking and Experimental Validation of Host-Factor and Direct-Acting Agent Combinations Against SARS-CoV-2
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, antiviral strategies for SARS-CoV-2 primarily target viral proteins like the main protease (Mpro), but resistance and limited efficacy remain concerns. Targeting host factors essential for viral replication offers a complementary, potentially resistance-resistant approach. Combining host-targeting agents (HTAs) with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) could yield synergistic effects, lower effective doses, and improve therapeutic outcomes, yet systematic exploration of such combinations is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of computationally identified host-factor-targeting agents and direct-acting antivirals will exhibit synergistic inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 replication in vitro, surpassing the efficacy of either agent alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 library and the DOCK3.7 algorithm to virtually screen for novel HTAs against host targets (e.g., TMPRSS2, cathepsin L) previously implicated in SARS-CoV-2 entry. Top-ranking compounds from this screen and known HTAs (e.g., calcilytics from ADH1 research) will be experimentally combined with known DAAs (e.g., nirmatrelvir, remdesivir) in cell-based antiviral assays (e.g., Vero E6-TMPRSS2). Synergy will be quantified using the Bliss independence model and dose-response matrix analysis. Promising synergistic pairs will be further characterized for cytotoxicity and mechanism.
