proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated set of synergistic HTA-DAA pairs, providing new leads for combination therapy against SARS-CoV-2. It will establish a computational-experimental pipeline for rapidly identifying antiviral combinations, applicable to future pandemic threats. The results will be disseminated via an update to the UCSF Drug Discovery Database, providing a public resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While Shoichet's lab has excelled in large-scale docking for single targets, this proposal uniquely integrates host-target and viral-target docking campaigns to rationally design combination therapies, a strategy not yet fully explored in their published work. The application of high-throughput synergy screening to computationally prioritized combinations from distinct target classes is novel.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of 69 Docking-Predicted Compounds as Broad-Spectrum Antivirals Targeting SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a conserved and essential viral enzyme, making it a prime target for antiviral development. While large-scale virtual docking campaigns, such as those pioneered by the Shoichet lab, have identified numerous potential Mpro inhibitors, the critical step of comprehensive experimental validation against live virus remains a bottleneck. Systematic screening of these computationally prioritized compounds is essential to translate in silico hits into viable chemical starting points for pandemic preparedness.
proposals.hypothesis: A subset of the 69 compounds, identified through structure-based docking against Mpro, will exhibit potent antiviral activity in cellular assays by inhibiting viral replication through direct protease inhibition.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-stage antiviral screen. First, all 69 compounds will be tested in a cell-based SARS-CoV-2 cytopathic effect (CPE) assay using Vero E6 or Calu-3 cells to quantify reduction in viral-induced cell death (EC50). Second, hits from the CPE assay will be counter-screened in a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based enzymatic assay against purified Mpro to confirm target engagement and determine IC50 values. Compounds showing concordant activity in both assays will be prioritized for follow-up dose-response and cytotoxicity (CC50) profiling to establish selectivity indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive experimental dataset linking a specific set of docking-predicted compounds to antiviral activity, validating or refining computational models for Mpro. It will identify novel chemotypes with confirmed activity against SARS-CoV-2, contributing concrete hit compounds to the open-source antiviral discovery pipeline for future coronavirus threats.
proposals.novelty: While many studies have screened large libraries, this proposal focuses on the systematic experimental triage of a discrete, computationally derived set from a specific docking campaign, creating a closed-loop validation of a targeted virtual screening methodology for a therapeutically critical target.

proposals.title: Structure-Based Discovery of Broad-Spectrum Antivirals Targeting Conserved Host-Viral Protein-Protein Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic underscored the need for broad-spectrum antivirals that are less susceptible to viral mutation. Pre-2026 studies, including the mapping of SARS-CoV-2-human protein-protein interactions (PPIs), have identified key host factors essential for viral replication. However, these maps are largely 2D, lacking the 3D structural information required for rational, structure-based drug design against these challenging PPI targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating AlphaFold2-Multimer predictions of high-confidence viral-host PPIs with ultra-large-scale molecular docking will identify novel, chemically tractable small molecules that disrupt these interfaces, yielding starting points for broad-spectrum antiviral development.
proposals.method: We will first use AlphaFold2-Multimer to generate 3D structural models for top-priority viral-host complexes from published SARS-CoV-2 and related coronavirus interactomes. These models will be curated and prepared for docking using the DOCK3.7 pipeline. We will then screen the Enamine REAL library (over 1 billion make-on-demand compounds) against the predicted binding pockets at host-viral interfaces, leveraging the computational infrastructure from our prior opioid receptor work. Top-ranking virtual hits will be experimentally tested for antiviral activity and target engagement in relevant cellular assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first large-scale, structure-based docking campaign against predicted host-viral PPI interfaces, generating a publicly accessible database of predicted complex structures and docking results. It will also yield novel chemical starting points for antiviral development that target host-essential pathways, potentially with broader efficacy against future coronaviruses and other viruses.
proposals.novelty: While AlphaFold2 has revolutionized structure prediction, its systematic application to model entire viral-host interactomes for drug discovery is nascent. Combining this with billion-molecule docking against these novel, computationally derived targets represents a significant and untested advance over traditional campaigns against single, well-characterized viral enzymes like Mpro.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Host-Derived Protein Binders to Neutralize SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease
proposals.motivation: Traditional antiviral discovery focuses on small molecules targeting viral proteins, but these can face resistance. An orthogonal strategy, inspired by natural host defense, is to engineer human proteins to bind and neutralize viral proteins, potentially offering higher specificity and lower toxicity. This 'inverse' approach remains largely unexplored computationally, especially for high-impact targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, a critical enzyme for viral replication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically docking a curated library of human protein domains against the SARS-CoV-2 Mpro structure, we can identify 'scaffold' domains with inherent, evolvable binding potential that can be engineered into high-affinity, neutralizing biologic inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 library and the DOCK3.7 suite to perform large-scale, rigid-body docking of a curated library of ~50,000 human protein domains (e.g., SH3, FN3, ankyrin repeats) against the substrate-binding cleft of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro (PDB: 6LU7). Top-ranking scaffolds will be subjected to flexible side-chain optimization using Rosetta. The most promising computational hits will be experimentally validated using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to measure binding to Mpro and a fluorescence-based enzymatic assay to test inhibition of protease activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a computational pipeline for the *de novo* discovery of host-derived antiviral protein scaffolds, moving beyond small molecules. It will deliver a shortlist of human protein domains with confirmed binding to Mpro, providing starting points for protein engineering campaigns. This contributes a novel framework for developing 'host-inspired' biologic therapeutics against current and future viral threats.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking against host proteins (e.g., GPCRs) is established, systematically docking the human proteome *against* a viral target to find engineered protein therapeutics is a novel inversion of the standard paradigm. It applies high-throughput computational methods, proven in small-molecule discovery, to the emerging field of de novo protein binder design.

proposals.title: Pan-Viral Proteome Docking: A Computational Screen for Broad-Spectrum Antiviral Scaffolds Against SARS-CoV-2 and Related Coronaviruses
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 pandemic highlighted the urgent need for broad-spectrum antivirals that can target conserved viral proteins across related coronaviruses. Traditional drug discovery often focuses on a single, high-profile target like the main protease (Mpro), potentially missing vulnerabilities in other essential viral proteins. A systematic, proteome-wide computational screen can identify novel chemical starting points against multiple targets, accelerating the discovery of first-in-class antivirals and preparing for future outbreaks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large-scale molecular docking against a curated set of structurally characterized proteins from the SARS-CoV-2 proteome and related sarbecoviruses will reveal novel, high-affinity small-molecule ligands for targets beyond Mpro, identifying chemical scaffolds with potential for broad-spectrum activity.
proposals.method: We will employ the validated docking pipeline and infrastructure from the Shoichet lab (e.g., DOCK 3.7+). First, we will prepare a target set of 8-10 high-resolution protein structures from the SARS-CoV-2 proteome (e.g., Mpro, PLpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp13 helicase, N protein RNA-binding domain). We will then screen an ultra-large library of ~15 million commercially available, drug-like molecules against each target in parallel on high-performance computing clusters. Top-ranking compounds from each target will be clustered by scaffold, analyzed for target-specific and cross-target binding patterns, and a prioritized subset will be selected for experimental validation in biochemical and antiviral assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A public database of docking poses and scores for millions of compounds against multiple coronavirus targets, serving as a community resource. 2) A set of experimentally validated hit compounds for under-explored viral proteins. 3) A map of chemotype-target interactions that reveals privileged scaffolds for coronavirus inhibition, informing future medicinal chemistry campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking against single targets is established, applying this scale and rigor simultaneously across an entire viral proteome to map polypharmacology and identify cross-reactive scaffolds is a novel and systematic approach to early-stage antiviral discovery.

proposals.title: Large-Scale Computational Profiling of Antiviral Compounds Against Human Off-Targets to Predict and Mitigate Adverse Effects
proposals.motivation: The rapid identification of antiviral compounds, such as SARS-CoV-2 Mpro inhibitors, is crucial for pandemic response. However, pre-clinical safety assessment remains a bottleneck, as unexpected off-target interactions with human proteins can cause severe adverse effects. Current computational approaches are limited to narrow target panels and lack integration with comprehensive side-effect ontologies, hindering systematic safety prediction early in discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large-scale molecular docking of antiviral compound libraries against a vastly expanded set of human protein structures, integrated with phenotypic side-effect data, will enable the accurate prediction of adverse effect profiles and reveal novel, safer chemotypes.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 library and the DOCK 3.7 algorithm to screen ~10 million commercially available molecules against a curated set of ~2,000 human protein structures from the PDB and AlphaFold2 database (pre-2026 v2.3). This expands far beyond typical panels like the 'druggable genome'. Off-target predictions will be computationally integrated with side-effect annotations from the SIDER and OFFSIDES databases using ontology mapping via the UMLS, creating a compound-side effect linkage network. Machine learning models (e.g., random forest) will be trained to predict adverse effect classes from docking scores and chemical descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, publicly accessible resource linking antiviral chemical space to a comprehensive human off-target and side-effect space. It will provide a method to prioritize compounds with lower predicted adverse effect burdens and reveal novel polypharmacology mechanisms underlying known side effects, directly informing safer antiviral design.
proposals.novelty: The scale of the docking campaign (thousands of human targets) and the systematic computational integration of structural off-target predictions with a formal side-effect ontology represents a significant advance over existing fragment-based or narrow-panel safety profiling methods.

proposals.title: Integrating ADME Predictions with Structure-Based Docking to Prioritize Clinically Relevant Off-Targets for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: Large-scale docking campaigns, like those pioneered by the Shoichet lab, identify thousands of potential off-target interactions for drug candidates, but most are pharmacologically irrelevant due to poor pharmacokinetic (PK) properties. This creates a critical bottleneck in translating computational hits into safe, effective therapies, particularly for antivirals like SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) inhibitors where rapid development is essential. A systematic method to filter docking results by predicted in vivo exposure is needed to focus experimental validation on the most clinically concerning off-targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating predicted ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties—specifically unbound plasma concentration (C_u) and tissue distribution—with structure-based docking scores will significantly improve the prediction of which off-target interactions are likely to manifest adverse effects in vivo.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 library and the DOCK3.7 algorithm to perform ultra-large library docking of known and proposed Mpro inhibitors against a panel of 300+ high-risk human off-targets (e.g., GPCRs, kinases, ion channels). For each compound, we will predict key ADME parameters (e.g., human hepatic clearance, volume of distribution, plasma protein binding) using established QSAR models (e.g., OPERA, SwissADME) and simulate a human steady-state pharmacokinetic profile. We will then develop a composite scoring function that weights the docking pose energy by the predicted time-averaged unbound concentration (f_u*C_avg) at the putative off-target site, ranking interactions by their plausible in vivo relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly accessible computational pipeline that merges structure-based docking with PK simulation to prioritize off-targets for experimental counterscreening. It will provide a focused, experimentally testable list of the most probable adverse effect mechanisms for Mpro inhibitors, directly informing safety pharmacology. The method will be generalizable to other therapeutic targets, enhancing the translational impact of virtual screening.
proposals.novelty: While ADME filters are commonly applied early in virtual screening for *primary* target activity, their systematic integration *post-hoc* to evaluate the clinical risk of *off-target* docking hits is a novel strategy. This moves beyond static affinity predictions to a dynamic, exposure-aware assessment of polypharmacology.

proposals.title: Network Propagation of Docked Ligands to Predict Off-Target and Side Effect Pathways for Antiviral Compounds
proposals.motivation: High-throughput docking, as pioneered by the Shoichet lab, excels at predicting primary target engagement but often fails to anticipate distal, systems-level effects leading to clinical side effects. This is a critical limitation for antiviral drug discovery, where compounds like Mpro inhibitors must be both potent and safe. Pre-2026, integrating large-scale docking results with human protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks to systematically map side effect pathways remains an underexplored opportunity.
proposals.hypothesis: Propagating the perturbation signal from computationally docked ligands through a human PPI network will identify novel off-target proteins and downstream biological pathways that correlate with known and predictable clinical side effects.
proposals.method: We will dock large commercial and virtual libraries (e.g., 10+ million compounds) against primary antiviral targets (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro) using the lab's established DOCK3.7/ZINC pipeline. Top-ranking hits will be used as seeds in a network propagation algorithm (e.g., Random Walk with Restart) on a high-confidence human PPI network (e.g., STRING or HuRI). The resulting 'influence scores' for all proteins in the network will be linked to side effect databases (e.g., SIDER, OFFSIDES) via gene-trait associations to rank predicted adverse effect pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational pipeline that bridges structure-based docking and systems biology to predict side effects *in silico*. It will generate testable hypotheses for off-targets of antiviral leads and provide a framework for prioritizing compounds with lower predicted systemic risk early in discovery.
proposals.novelty: While network propagation is used in disease genetics, its application to propagate the effects of *docked* small molecules—treating docking scores as node-specific perturbation inputs—to predict clinical side effects is a novel synthesis of the lab's core methodologies.

proposals.title: Proteome-Scale Inverse Docking: A Computational Platform for Predicting Polypharmacology and Off-Target Effects of Approved Drugs
proposals.motivation: Understanding the polypharmacology of drugs—their binding to multiple protein targets—is crucial for predicting efficacy, toxicity, and repurposing opportunities. Current virtual screening is target-centric, screening millions of compounds against a single protein. A systematic, scalable inverse approach, docking all approved drugs against all structurally characterized human binding sites, would provide an unprecedented map of drug-protein interactions, directly addressing the challenge of off-target prediction and novel therapeutic hypothesis generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large-scale, structure-based inverse docking of approved drugs against a comprehensive set of human protein binding pockets will reveal novel, high-confidence off-targets and repurposing opportunities that are not apparent from sequence or phenotypic data alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the DOCK 3.7 algorithm and the infrastructure from the ZINC database and the DUDE-Z benchmark. The method involves: 1) Curating a non-redundant set of all high-resolution, druggable human protein structures and homology models from the PDB and AlphaFold DB (pre-2026 v2.3), focusing on orthosteric sites. 2) Preparing a library of ~5,000 approved and investigational drugs from ZINC and DrugBank. 3) Implementing massively parallel inverse docking on high-performance computing clusters using a modified DOCK workflow optimized for proteome-scale throughput. 4) Validating top predictions using existing bioactivity data (ChEMBL) and prioritizing a subset for experimental testing in binding or functional assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly accessible database of proteome-wide docking scores for approved drugs, serving as a foundational resource for chemical biology and drug discovery. It will establish a generalizable computational pipeline for systematic off-target identification and directly yield new, testable hypotheses for drug repurposing and the mechanistic explanation of clinical side effects.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale docking of compound libraries against single targets is established, inverting this paradigm to screen all drugs against the entire structurally characterized proteome is a novel, systematic approach to polypharmacology prediction. This scale and directionality move beyond retrospective analysis to a prospective, hypothesis-generating platform.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Selective Ligands via Multi-Target Constrained Generative Docking
proposals.motivation: Current virtual screening and generative models primarily focus on maximizing affinity for a primary target, often neglecting off-target activity, which is a major cause of clinical attrition and adverse effects. A method to explicitly design molecules with minimal predicted activity against a panel of side-effect targets would directly address this critical safety issue early in drug discovery. This is particularly relevant for antiviral targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, where rapid design of selective compounds is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative model conditioned on a primary target's active site and constrained by a multi-target docking-based penalty function can produce novel chemical matter with high predicted on-target activity and low predicted off-target activity against a defined panel of antitargets.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) or a graph-based generative model where the conditioning vector encodes the 3D structure of the primary target's binding site, derived from docking poses. The generative process will be guided by a multi-objective loss function combining (1) a reward for high predicted docking scores (using Glide or AutoDock Vina) against the primary target, and (2) a penalty for high predicted scores against a curated panel of antitargets (e.g., hERG, 5-HT2B, opioid receptors). We will validate the approach by generating and experimentally testing compounds designed against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, with off-target panels informed by prior antiviral drug safety profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for the inverse design of selective ligands, moving beyond single-target optimization. It will produce a new, openly accessible library of Mpro-targeted compounds with computationally prioritized selectivity profiles. The method will provide a generalizable framework for incorporating multi-target safety constraints into generative chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While generative models and docking are established, their integration for explicit multi-target *de-prioritization*—framing selectivity as an inverse design problem—is novel. This approach inverts the typical screening paradigm by generating molecules that are *predicted to be inactive* against undesired targets from the outset.

proposals.title: Extending Large-Scale Docking to Non-Protein Biomolecular Targets: A Framework for Predicting Off-Target Interactions with Nucleic Acids and Metabolites
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale virtual screening frameworks, including those pioneered by the Shoichet lab, are almost exclusively optimized for protein targets. However, small-molecule drugs can have significant off-target effects by binding to nucleic acids, lipids, or metabolites, contributing to toxicity and side effects. A systematic, scalable computational method to predict such interactions is lacking, limiting our ability to comprehensively profile compound safety in silico.
proposals.hypothesis: That the high-throughput docking and scoring infrastructure developed for proteins can be adapted and validated to predict small-molecule binding to structured nucleic acid elements (e.g., riboswitches, G-quadruplexes) and key metabolic cofactors, providing a crucial expansion of off-target prediction capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the ZINC library and the DOCK 3.7 algorithm to handle nucleic acid targets by parameterizing scoring functions for nucleobase interactions and phosphate backbone electrostatics, based on pre-2026 force fields like AM1-BCC for charges. A curated library of ~10 million commercially available molecules will be docked against a benchmark set of non-protein targets with known binders, including the FMN riboswitch, purine riboswitches, and NAD(P)H. Success will be evaluated by the enrichment of known binders in top-ranked poses and by experimental validation of high-ranking novel hits using biophysical assays (e.g., SPR, DSF) for selected targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly accessible database of docking predictions for small molecules against non-protein biomolecular targets, directly extending the lab's existing database for protein docking. It will provide a new computational tool for early-stage toxicity and polypharmacology prediction, moving beyond the proteome. The validated framework and benchmark sets will be a resource for the chemical biology and drug discovery communities.
proposals.novelty: While nucleic acid docking exists in niche applications, its integration into a massive, automated, library-scale screening pipeline akin to high-throughput protein docking is unprecedented. This proposal systematically applies a proven, scale-oriented methodology from the Shoichet lab to a fundamentally new class of biomolecular targets.

proposals.title: Systematic Computational and Experimental Evaluation of Transient Allosteric Pockets in Viral Targets for Drug Discovery
proposals.motivation: The prevailing view in structure-based drug design is that transient, cryptic, or temperature-sensitive allosteric sites are poor targets due to their low stability and difficulty in identification. This has potentially led to the neglect of a vast, untapped landscape of regulatory sites, particularly in dynamic viral proteins like SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro). Challenging this dogma is crucial for expanding the druggable proteome, especially for targets where orthosteric sites are highly conserved or prone to resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Transient allosteric sites, detectable through enhanced molecular dynamics simulations and validated by temperature-sensitive crystallography, can be targeted by small molecules to achieve potent and selective inhibition, providing a viable strategy for antiviral discovery.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 ultra-large library and the DOCK3.7 suite to perform ensemble docking against conformational ensembles of SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and other viral targets, generated from extensive (µs-scale) molecular dynamics simulations at multiple temperatures. Cryptic site detection will use algorithms like TRAPP and CAVER. Top-ranking compounds from virtual screens will be tested experimentally using a fluorescence-based enzymatic assay, followed by characterization via room-temperature and cryo-crystallography to directly visualize ligand binding in transient pockets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven test of the 'transient site irrelevance' hypothesis, generating a public database of docking results and experimental validations for transient sites. It will establish a methodological framework for targeting dynamic pockets and could deliver novel allosteric chemotypes against viral targets of high biomedical importance.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges a core assumption in the field by applying Shoichet's established ultra-large library docking and experimental validation pipeline—previously used for stable GPCR and enzyme pockets—to the explicit problem of transient, temperature-sensitive site druggability, a combination not yet systematically explored.

proposals.title: Room-Temperature Cryo-EM Reveals Cryptic Allosteric Sites in SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) for Novel Antiviral Discovery
proposals.motivation: Recent cryo-EM studies (pre-2026) have demonstrated that proteins exhibit greater conformational heterogeneity at room temperature compared to traditional cryogenic conditions, revealing transient binding pockets. For high-value antiviral targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, these 'hidden' sites represent a vast, untapped reservoir for allosteric drug discovery. Characterizing their functional potential could unlock new chemotypes and mechanisms to combat viral resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Cryptic binding sites in Mpro, resolved only in room-temperature cryo-EM structures, are functionally relevant allosteric pockets whose occupancy by small molecules can modulate protease activity.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Shoichet lab's expertise in large-scale docking against experimental structures. First, we will curate or generate room-temperature cryo-EM structures of Mpro from public databases or collaborators. Using the DOCK 3.7 algorithm, we will screen the lab's 14+ million compound library against these newly revealed cryptic sites. Top-ranking virtual hits will be experimentally tested for Mpro inhibition and allosteric mechanism using enzymatic assays and hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct functional link between thermally revealed protein dynamics and allosteric pharmacology for a critical antiviral target. It will produce a set of validated, novel chemotypes targeting Mpro allosterically, providing starting points for drug development and a methodological blueprint for targeting other viral proteins.
proposals.novelty: While room-temperature cryo-EM is emerging, its application for systematic, large-scale virtual screening and functional validation of the revealed cryptic sites is unexplored. This moves beyond static structural observation to active drug discovery against dynamic targets.

proposals.title: Decoupling Binding from Function: A High-Throughput Docking and Experimental Framework to Predict Functional Agonism and Antagonism for SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease
proposals.motivation: High-throughput virtual screening, a cornerstone of modern drug discovery, primarily identifies compounds that bind a target. However, as demonstrated in Shoichet's work on GPCRs like opioid and calcium-sensing receptors, binding does not equate to a desired functional effect (agonist vs. antagonist). This is a critical bottleneck, especially for targets like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, where inhibitors are sought. The field lacks generalizable computational strategies to predict functional outcome from docking poses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable features of ligand-target complexes beyond simple binding affinity—such as pose stability, interaction fingerprints, and allosteric network perturbations—can be used to build machine learning models that distinguish functional agonists from antagonists for enzymatic targets like Mpro.
proposals.method: We will leverage the ZINC20 library and the DOCK3.7 suite to dock millions of diverse compounds against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro. For a curated training set of known Mpro binders with experimentally validated functional effects (inactive binders, covalent inhibitors, allosteric modulators), we will compute pose-based descriptors (e.g., interaction energy terms, molecular dynamics-derived stability metrics, and graph-based signatures of induced conformational changes). These will train a random forest classifier to predict functional class. Top computational predictions will be validated using high-throughput enzymatic activity assays and cellular antiviral assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible dataset linking Mpro docking poses to functional outcomes and a validated predictive model. It will provide a concrete methodological framework to move beyond binding predictions to functional predictions in virtual screening, directly applicable to antiviral discovery. The approach will be generalizable to other pharmaceutically relevant enzymatic targets.
proposals.novelty: While pose-based activity prediction is emerging for GPCRs, its systematic application to a viral protease target like Mpro, with a focus on distinguishing non-functional binders from inhibitors, is novel. The integration of large-scale docking with functional machine learning classifiers specifically for this purpose addresses a recognized but unsolved gap in computational chemistry.

proposals.title: A Polypharmacology-Centric Docking Framework: Systematic Off-Target Prediction and Multi-Target Optimization for Antiviral Leads
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 drug discovery, especially for antivirals like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro inhibitors, often focuses on maximizing single-target selectivity. However, clinical efficacy and side effects are frequently governed by polypharmacology. The field lacks systematic, large-scale computational frameworks that treat multi-target engagement as the default starting point for lead optimization, limiting our ability to rationally design compounds with desired polypharmacological profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the inherent polypharmacology of small molecules through large-scale, structure-based docking against diverse target libraries, we can more accurately predict biological activity profiles and identify lead compounds with optimized multi-target signatures for antiviral efficacy and reduced toxicity.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the ZINC20 library and the DOCK 3.8 algorithm, as used in the Shoichet lab's opioid receptor work, to dock millions of commercially available compounds against a curated 'antiviral polypharmacology panel'. This panel will include primary targets (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 Mpro, other viral proteases) and human off-targets implicated in toxicity (e.g., GPCRs, kinases, ion channels). We will develop a multi-objective scoring function to rank compounds not by single-target affinity, but by their ability to hit a desired combination of targets while avoiding adverse ones. Experimental validation will follow using the lab's established high-throughput screening and biochemical assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a publicly accessible database of predicted polypharmacology profiles for millions of compounds against virologically and pharmacologically relevant targets; 2) novel, experimentally validated lead compounds for antiviral development with characterized multi-target mechanisms; 3) a new computational paradigm where selectivity is engineered *from* a polypharmacology baseline.
proposals.novelty: While off-target prediction exists, this proposal inverts the paradigm: instead of seeking selective drugs and checking for off-targets, we start by assuming broad polypharmacology and computationally optimize for a specific, multi-target profile. This systematic, large-scale approach applied to antiviral discovery is distinct from prior fragment-based or single-target efforts.

proposals.title: A Network Science Analysis of the SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) Chemical Space to Identify Polypharmacological Communities and Repurposing Candidates
proposals.motivation: High-throughput virtual screening, as exemplified by the Shoichet lab's docking of billions of molecules, generates vast, disconnected lists of predicted actives. This atomistic view overlooks the systemic organization of chemical space and potential polypharmacological relationships. A network-based framework is needed to map the global topology of drug-target interactions, revealing communities of compounds that share target profiles and identifying key 'hub' molecules with privileged scaffolds for broad antiviral activity, particularly against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and related viral targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the drug-target landscape for SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and related coronaviral proteases as a bipartite network will reveal densely connected communities of chemically diverse compounds that represent distinct polypharmacological regimes and highlight privileged scaffolds with high network centrality as superior starting points for broad-spectrum antiviral development.
proposals.method: We will construct a bipartite network from the Shoichet lab's ultra-large library docking results against SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and related viral targets (e.g., SARS-CoV-1 Mpro, MERS-CoV Mpro). Nodes will be compounds and protein structures; edges will be strong docking scores. We will apply community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method) to identify clusters of compounds with similar target profiles. Network centrality measures (degree, betweenness) will rank compounds by their topological importance. We will validate predictions by experimentally testing high-centrality compounds from distinct communities in enzymatic and cellular antiviral assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-level map of the Mpro inhibitor landscape, moving beyond single-target rankings. It will deliver a prioritized set of chemically diverse lead compounds with predicted polypharmacology, and establish a general network science framework for analyzing ultra-large virtual screening datasets to uncover hidden systemic relationships.
proposals.novelty: While network pharmacology exists for known drug-target interactions, applying it prospectively to the billion-scale *virtual* chemical space generated by docking is novel. This approach uniquely identifies communities and hub compounds based on predicted binding landscapes before experimental testing, offering a new paradigm for lead prioritization.

proposals.title: Linking Docking-Predicted Antiviral Compounds to Host Transcriptional Signatures for COVID-19 Drug Repurposing
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) remains a critical antiviral target, but identifying compounds that modulate the host response is crucial for effective therapy. Pre-2026, large-scale docking against targets like Mpro has identified promising chemotypes, but their phenotypic effects and relevance to the disease state are unclear. Connecting these target-specific predictions to systems-level gene expression signatures of infection would prioritize compounds likely to reverse the pathogenic host cell state, moving beyond simple target binding.
proposals.hypothesis: Compounds predicted by structure-based docking to bind SARS-CoV-2 Mpro can be prioritized for experimental validation by identifying those whose predicted transcriptional signatures, derived from existing perturbation data, inversely correlate with gene expression signatures from SARS-CoV-2-infected cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Shoichet lab's expertise and databases for large-scale docking, starting with their existing libraries of millions of compounds docked against Mpro. Top-ranked virtual hits will be connected to gene expression signatures using the LINCS L1000 database and the Connectivity Map (CMap) resource (pre-2026). We will compute signature similarity using the weighted connectivity score (WTCS) or a similar metric to identify compounds whose transcriptional responses are anticorrelated with signatures from publicly available SARS-CoV-2 infection transcriptomics datasets (e.g., from primary airway epithelia). This computational triage will yield a shortlist of high-priority Mpro-docked compounds for experimental validation in viral replication assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly accessible dataset linking docking scores against a key viral target to host transcriptional response profiles. It will provide a prioritized list of repurposing candidates for COVID-19 with a dual rationale: direct antiviral action via Mpro inhibition and host-modulating effects. The integrated methodology establishes a generalizable framework for connecting target-centric virtual screening to disease-relevant phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: While docking and signature-based repurposing exist in parallel, their direct integration for antiviral discovery is not routine. This approach is novel in using gene expression signatures as a critical filter to select from thousands of high-scoring docking hits, focusing validation efforts on compounds with a systems-level link to the disease pathology.

proposals.title: Ultra-Large-Scale Docking of a 10-Million-Compound Bioactive Library Against SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease (Mpro) to Identify Novel Antiviral Chemotypes
proposals.motivation: The SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) is a validated, conserved antiviral target, but approved drug libraries are limited in chemical diversity. Pre-2026, ultra-large-scale docking has proven successful for targets like GPCRs, yet its application to viral proteases has been restricted to smaller, focused libraries. Expanding screening to the vast chemical space of commercially available bioactive compounds could uncover novel, potent, and drug-like Mpro inhibitors with immediate translational potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Ultra-large-scale docking of a multi-million compound library of purchasable bioactive molecules against the SARS-CoV-2 Mpro active site will identify novel, high-affinity chemotypes distinct from known peptidomimetic scaffolds, which can be experimentally validated as antiviral leads.
proposals.method: We will apply the validated ZINC20 library's 'in-stock' subset of ~10 million 3D-purchasable compounds. Docking will be performed using the FRED and HYBRID algorithms from the OpenEye toolkits, leveraging a high-resolution Mpro structure (e.g., PDB: 6LU7) prepared with the Schrödinger Suite. The computational pipeline, scaled using the Shoichet lab's established distributed computing infrastructure (e.g., as used for the 14-million-compound opioid receptor screen), will generate ranked hit lists. Top-scoring compounds will be prioritized using interaction fingerprint analysis and medicinal chemistry filters before purchase and experimental validation in Mpro enzymatic and cell-based antiviral assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, ultra-large-scale docking dataset for Mpro against a commercially accessible chemical library, identifying new lead series for coronavirus antivirals. It will demonstrate the generalizability of the ultra-large-scale docking paradigm from human receptors to viral enzymes, providing a blueprint for rapid response to future pandemic threats.
proposals.novelty: While the Shoichet lab has docked massive libraries against GPCRs, applying this scale and methodology to a viral protease target with a structurally distinct active site is novel. The focus on the 'purchasable bioactive' chemical space, rather than approved drugs or fully virtual compounds, significantly increases the probability of identifying novel, immediately testable leads.

ideas.title: TPP-Guided Dual-Strategy Library for Optimization and Scaffold De-risking
ideas.description: This approach first formalizes a target product profile (TPP) based on the initial hit's properties and the project goals. It then designs two complementary sub-libraries in parallel. The first is a traditional focused library for structure-activity relationship (SAR) exploration around the original chemotypes. The second is a scaffold-hopping library, generated using computational methods to propose novel core structures that are synthetically tractable and predicted to fulfill the TPP while minimizing the potential liabilities of the original series. This dual-strategy systematically balances local optimization with the exploration of new chemical space to avoid premature convergence on a suboptimal lead series.

ideas.title: Version-Controlled Collaborative Mapping of HCV Polymerase Inhibitor Binding Landscapes
ideas.description: This project establishes a Git-like informatics framework specifically designed for the collaborative discovery and characterization of HCV polymerase inhibitors targeting multiple sites. Every experimental compound profile—including binding affinity, resistance mutations, structural data, and functional assays—is treated as a 'commit,' enabling researchers to branch (fork) from any point to explore new chemical series or binding hypotheses. This creates a version-controlled, non-linear map of the inhibitor landscape, where merging data branches can reveal unexpected correlations between distinct binding sites, predict cooperative inhibitor combinations, and systematically trace the structural evolution of resistance, accelerating the identification of robust therapeutic strategies.

ideas.title: Designing multi-target probe 'cocktails' using ecological network stability principles
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that highly selective single-target probes are inherently superior for biological discovery. By drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology and ecosystem management—where successful restoration relies on introducing stable networks of interacting species, not single keystone organisms—we adapt the solution of network stability modeling. The adapted idea is to embrace polypharmacology by designing and validating multi-target probe sets, or 'cocktails,' where the polypharmacological profile of each component is intentionally characterized. The core of the research would involve modeling the pharmacological interactions within the probe set against the target interaction network of a disease state, aiming to validate that the collective, multi-target action produces a more resilient and therapeutically relevant phenotypic modulation, thereby demonstrating the utility of deliberately non-selective probe sets for probing complex biology.

ideas.title: Adapting Cancer Synthetic Lethality to HCV Host-Target Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'synthetic lethality' concept from oncology to HCV drug discovery. In cancer, when a primary oncogenic mutation is hard to drug, a secondary, complementary host dependency is targeted. Analogously, for HCV, we invert the screening focus from viral proteins to host factors, but with a specific strategic twist: we aim to discover compounds targeting human host factors that become synthetically lethal in the context of HCV infection. This involves first mapping the network of host factors HCV depends on, then identifying their complementary partner proteins in essential cellular pathways. A high-throughput screen would then seek inhibitors of these partner proteins, which should selectively kill or cripple HCV-infected hepatocytes while having minimal effect on uninfected cells, potentially leading to a novel antiviral strategy with a high barrier to resistance.

ideas.description: This approach integrates the design of a small, focused library from initial hits with a structured community review system. Each compound synthesized from the focused library is added to a public wiki, where experimental data from testing is continuously reported. The expert community then annotates these results, identifying structure-activity relationships, synthetic liabilities, and promising new directions, which directly inform the next iteration of focused library design. This creates a virtuous, accelerated feedback loop where chemical probe optimization is guided by collective intelligence and real-time data curation.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Drug Candidates via Pareto Frontier Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-objective optimization approach from aerospace engineering to the virtual screening pipeline. In aerospace, designers use Pareto frontiers to navigate the trade-off between weight and strength. Here, we apply the same principle to balance the dual objectives of biological potency and synthetic accessibility for drug candidates. The adapted solution involves calculating a Pareto frontier during screening to identify the set of non-dominated compounds—those where you cannot improve one objective (e.g., potency) without worsening the other (e.g., synthesizability). This provides a transparent, ranked portfolio of lead candidates, enabling chemists to make informed decisions based on the explicit trade-off landscape between desired activity and practical synthetic feasibility.

ideas.title: A Contextual Framework for Probe Utility: Redefining 'Quality' Beyond Potency and Deprecation
ideas.description: This research challenges the conventional, potency-centric probe assessment by proposing that 'quality' is a multi-dimensional, context-dependent property. It systematically evaluates probes traditionally deemed 'poor-quality'—particularly those with lower potency but higher specificity—not for deprecation, but for their utility as specialized tools. The fused idea develops a decision framework that maps probes to specific experimental contexts (e.g., validating assay specificity, probing compensatory pathways, or serving as negative controls), thereby transforming the probe selection process from a search for a single 'best' molecule to the strategic assignment of the most informative tool for a given biological question.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Algorithm for Focused Chemical Space Exploration
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from evolutionary algorithms in computer science to the problem of focused library design. The analogous field uses an iterative process of selection, crossover, and mutation on a population of candidates to converge on an optimized solution. Adapted here, the initial screening hits are used as a starting population. An algorithm defines their chemical features as genes, then performs virtual crossover and mutation to generate a new, focused generation of compounds. This child library is designed to explore the most promising regions of chemical space around the original hits, balancing exploration of novel combinations with exploitation of known successful chemotypes, before being synthesized and tested, creating an efficient, directed optimization cycle.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Focused Library Design via Iterative Human-AI Annotation Loops
ideas.description: This research direction integrates crowdsourced human annotation from platforms like Foldit directly into the focused library design pipeline. Instead of a one-way flow from crowd data to a static library, the process becomes an iterative loop: initial probe data is annotated by the citizen science community to identify promising chemical motifs and rule-of-five violations; these human-identified 'chemotype seeds' are used to generate a small, focused virtual library via generative or combinatorial AI models. This proposed library is then presented back to the crowd for iterative feedback on synthesizability, novelty, and perceived bioactivity, refining the AI's design parameters. The final output is a highly curated, synthetically accessible library where each compound is pre-vetted by human intuition, dramatically increasing the efficiency of the design-make-test-analyze cycle.

ideas.description: This redesigned project establishes a sequential validation pipeline for the top virtual screening hits. First, biochemical inhibition of HCV polymerase is confirmed in a purified enzyme assay. Concurrently, compounds undergo a parallel cell-based antiviral efficacy screen in a HCV replicon system and a mini-panel of early in vitro ADMET assays (e.g., metabolic stability in liver microsomes, Caco-2 permeability, cytotoxicity). Only compounds demonstrating both biochemical potency, replicon inhibition, and acceptable early drug-like properties advance to subsequent in vivo pharmacokinetic and efficacy studies in an appropriate animal model, ensuring resources are focused on the most promising leads.

ideas.title: Post-Approval Surveillance for Host-Targeted Antivirals Using a Pharmacovigilance Framework
ideas.description: This research direction applies established clinical pharmacovigilance methodologies to the post-approval monitoring of host-targeting antivirals, such as those developed for HCV. It inverts the traditional focus from viral targets to establishing a surveillance network that systematically collects and analyzes reports of reduced drug efficacy ('probe failures') in treated populations, analogous to adverse event reporting. This system is designed to detect early signs of viral escape through alternative host factor usage or host pathway adaptation, which are unique failure modes for this drug class. The resulting data feed would validate host target essentiality in humans and guide the development of next-generation combination therapies to preempt resistance.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Virtual Screening with Dynamic Synthetic Feasibility Filtering
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static scoring by embedding a dynamic synthetic feasibility assessment directly into the virtual screening workflow. It integrates user-defined lab profiles (equipment, technical expertise, in-stock building blocks) with real-time reagent sourcing data to evaluate compound synthesizability within a specific practical context. For each high-potential compound, a retrosynthetic analysis is performed to generate and rank feasible routes based on the available context, prioritizing compounds with viable, lab-specific synthesis plans. This ensures the final hit list is not only potent and synthetically accessible in theory, but is practically makeable by the specific research team executing the project.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Evidence-Weighted Scoring Framework for Chemical Probe Assessment and Prioritization
ideas.description: This project develops an open, computational platform that dynamically scores and ranks chemical probes based on a continuously updating evidence base, rather than static minimum criteria. The framework algorithmically weights different types of characterization data (e.g., selectivity, cellular activity, structural confirmation) according to their empirically demonstrated predictive value for downstream research utility, using historical project outcomes for training. Each probe receives a confidence-scored profile that automatically updates as new public or contributor data is added, creating a living assessment that mitigates checklist complacency and adapts to advancing scientific standards.

ideas.title: Probe Validation Kit: The Lateral Flow Assay for Molecular Tools
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principles of the lateral flow assay from medical diagnostics to create a minimal validation kit for a single molecular probe. The analogous solution from point-of-care testing provides a model for a fully integrated, equipment-free, and visually read system. The adapted research idea is to develop a self-contained, low-cost, disposable cartridge where all validation reagents are pre-dried in zones on a paper or polymer matrix. To validate a probe's activity, a researcher adds a standard buffer to rehydrate the system and then applies their probe solution. The device would guide the fluid via capillary action through zones containing the necessary target and reporter systems, culminating in a simple color change—akin to a control line and test line—to confirm the probe's specific functionality, making probe validation accessible to any lab.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Version Control for Probe Lineage Tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from evolutionary biology's phylogenetics to the problem of tracking probe characterization data. In phylogenetics, trees map the lineage and divergence of species from ancestors, tracking mutations and shared traits. The adapted research idea applies this model to create a 'Phylogenetic Version Control' system for probes. Each probe profile is treated as a taxon in an evolutionary tree, with its experimental characterization data (e.g., binding curves, specificity matrices) as heritable traits. The system visually maps forks (design divergences) and merges (combination of beneficial features) within probe families, enabling researchers to trace the contribution history and evolutionary pathway of successful probe variants. This goes beyond linear Git history to provide a branched, causal understanding of how design changes lead to functional outcomes.

Instead of hard-coded links, it uses a formal chemical probe ontology to create semantic mappings between entities across resources, enabling intelligent querying and inference. A dedicated maintenance pipeline employs versioned identifier mapping services and automated consistency checks to monitor and repair links as source databases evolve. This approach ensures sustained data integrity, reduces link rot, and provides clear provenance for integrated knowledge.

ideas.title: A Pre-registered Framework for Objectively Evaluating the Contextual Utility of Low-Performance Assay Components
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous, pre-registered pipeline to test whether assay components objectively classified as low-quality have reproducible utility. First, a multi-parameter quality metric is defined and applied to a large, held-out probe set to create fixed quality tiers, preventing post-hoc re-categorization. These pre-classified 'low-tier' probes are then evaluated as potential negative controls or context-specific signals across blinded experimental batches and independent analytical workflows. The outcome is a principled, falsifiable assessment of whether objectively poor-performing elements have systematic value, moving beyond anecdotal claims.

ideas.title: Adapting Galaxy Zoo's consensus model for crowdsourced probe data curation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the astronomy field's Galaxy Zoo project to the problem of curating large volumes of probe data. In Galaxy Zoo, the classification of vast numbers of galaxy images was achieved by distributing tasks to a broad citizen scientist community and using a consensus model to aggregate individual inputs into a reliable dataset. This approach can be transferred by creating a platform where volunteers annotate probe data (e.g., identifying features in environmental sensor data or labeling cellular structures in bio-images). Each data unit is reviewed by multiple users, and a dynamic consensus algorithm—which weights contributions based on user performance and agreement with the crowd—synthesizes the inputs to produce curated, high-quality labels. This method leverages crowd wisdom while mitigating individual error, efficiently generating annotated datasets for machine learning or direct analysis.

ideas.title: Probe Performance Fingerprint Kit: A Quantitative Benchmarking System for Single Probes
ideas.description: This kit provides not only the reagents to validate a single probe's activity but also a standardized set of control samples and a simple digital analysis framework to generate a quantitative performance profile. Users can measure key metrics such as signal-to-noise, dynamic range, and specificity under their specific lab conditions, enabling direct comparison to ideal benchmarks. This transforms validation from a binary check into a diagnostic tool for optimizing protocols, troubleshooting failures, and ensuring reproducible, high-quality results across different laboratories and applications.

ideas.title: Adopt Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Protocols from Clinical Research
ideas.description: This idea proposes transferring the rigorous evidence-assessment frameworks from clinical research to solve the problem of unverified and irreproducible claims about scientific probes. In clinical medicine, systematic review protocols and evidence-grading systems (e.g., PRISMA, GRADE) are used to objectively evaluate the strength and quality of evidence supporting a treatment's efficacy, flagging biases and uncertainties. Adapted to probe validation, researchers would implement a structured, pre-registered process for compiling evidence on a probe's utility. Each claim would be rated using a formal hierarchy of evidence (e.g., independent replication > original characterization > anecdotal report), resulting in a dynamically updated, version-controlled evidence profile. This profile would clearly flag the certainty of claims, document retractions, and provide a standardized, auditable record to guide rigorous probe selection and use.

ideas.title: Mandate a Procedural Correctness Layer to structurally enforce robust probe use
ideas.description: This research program first identifies the cognitive and methodological roots of probe misuse, as in the original idea. Its core innovation is to then codify these insights into a lightweight, domain-specific Procedural Correctness Layer (PCL)—a checklist-driven or tool-assisted protocol integrated into the experimental pipeline. The PCL mandates explicit documentation of probe validation, interpretation boundaries, and alternative hypothesis checks before results can be finalized. This creates an auditable trail, transforming training concepts into enforceable practice, thereby directly reducing misuse by design rather than just by awareness.

ideas.title: Question if all poor-quality probes should be deprecated

ideas.title: Probe Characterization Data Provenance System with Content-Aware Versioning
ideas.description: This system implements granular version control for scientific instrument data by separating large, immutable binary datasets from the metadata and analysis code that define their context and processing. Each dataset from a probe is stored in a content-addressable repository, with a versioned manifest linking it to the exact instrument software settings, calibration files, and environmental logs. Analysis pipelines, which generate derived metrics and characterizations, are themselves versioned using Git, creating a complete, reproducible audit trail from raw data to published results. This allows for true forking and comparison of entire characterization methodologies, not just the final profiles, while efficiently handling binary data.

ideas.title: A Cross-Validated, Uncertainty-Quantified Framework for Calibrating Crowdsourced Scientific Annotations
ideas.description: This redesigned system first collects raw annotations from platforms like Foldit or Zooniverse but does not treat them as direct labels. Instead, it employs a dynamic, Bayesian calibration layer that continuously compares crowd consensus against a rotating set of expert-validated anchor tasks. Each annotator receives a reliability score that weights their future contributions, and the system models the uncertainty for every annotation. The final output is a curated dataset with probabilistic labels and quantified confidence intervals, enabling robust model training that is explicitly aware of and resilient to the noise inherent in public sourcing.

ideas.title: A Gamified, Multi-Tiered Pipeline for Continuous Probe Data Curation and Validation
ideas.description: This fused idea designs a structured pipeline that integrates mass-scale crowdsourced annotation from citizen scientists with a formalized, expert-driven review and governance process. Citizen contributors engage through gamified platforms to perform initial tagging, anomaly detection, and data enrichment on probe information, generating a high-volume stream of candidate updates. This output is then systematically channeled into a community wiki framework, where domain experts and veteran contributors oversee structured review rounds, validate findings, and synthesize annotations into authoritative, versioned entries. The synergy creates a continuous, scalable loop of data refinement that leverages broad participation for coverage and dynamism while ensuring scientific rigor and reliability through curated governance.

ideas.title: Epistemic Context Mapping for Contested Scientific Claims
ideas.description: This system maps the landscape of claims about probe utility by explicitly modeling not just the claims themselves, but the underlying evidence, methodological assumptions, and theoretical perspectives that inform them. It treats claims as nodes in a dynamic network linked to supporting/contradictory evidence and contextual metadata, tracking how their perceived utility shifts with new publications, retractions, and critiques. The output is not a binary fact-check but an interactive, navigable map that highlights areas of robust consensus, active debate, and evidentiary weakness, allowing researchers to understand the epistemic status of a claim within its full scholarly context.

ideas.title: Adapting Aviation Safety Reporting Systems for Scientific Probe Failure Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from aviation safety, where confidential, non-punitive reporting systems (like NASA's ASRS) are used to collect voluntary incident reports to identify systemic risks. The analogous problem of identifying latent hazards in complex systems parallels the problem of unidentified probe failures corrupting scientific literature. The adapted research idea is to create a similar reporting portal for science, where researchers can confidentially report detailed technical accounts of probe failures (e.g., an antibody not working as specified, a software tool producing erroneous outputs). By analyzing aggregated, anonymized reports, the system could identify patterns, common failure modes, and problematic batches or versions, leading to the issuance of community-wide technical safety alerts. This moves beyond simple post-publication commenting to a structured, safety-engineering approach for monitoring the health of the scientific toolchain.

ideas.title: Treat Probe Misuse as a Cognitive 'Contamination' Problem, Solvable by Procedural Debiasing
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy to the problem of cognitive bias and contextual contamination in forensic science. In that field, solutions like 'sequential unmasking' were developed to separate objective technical analysis from biasing contextual information. Adapting this solution, the research would focus on designing and testing new training protocols and experimental workflows for using diagnostic probes in machine learning. The core adaptation is to treat probe analysis as a two-stage process: a blinded, hypothesis-agnostic technical assessment followed by contextual interpretation. This aims to mitigate cognitive biases (e.g., confirmation bias) and experimental design flaws by making the analyst's initial judgment independent of the expected result, thereby reframing probe misuse primarily as a tractable training and procedural problem rather than just a lack of resources or technical skill.

ideas.title: An Incentive-Aligned Registry for Rigorous Negative Results and Methodological Post-Mortems
ideas.description: This system establishes a centralized, curated registry specifically for documenting and crediting rigorous negative results, failed replications, and detailed methodological 'post-mortems' from research projects. To overcome reporting bias, it integrates with funders to make deposition a grant closure requirement and with journals to offer 'sister publication' links, providing formal academic credit for such reports. The registry employs structured reporting templates to ensure methodological detail and uses algorithmic monitoring of subsequent citations to track the registry's impact on correcting the scientific record. This transforms failure reporting from a voluntary altruistic act into an incentivized component of standard research practice.

ideas.title: Adversarial Governance and Prediction Markets for Probe Claim Review
ideas.description: This system replaces simple community curation with a structured adversarial process. Every substantive claim or probe update must undergo a formal, incentivized challenge from a designated 'Red Team' tasked with finding flaws or counter-evidence. Contributor reputation is tracked separately for proposing claims and for successfully critiquing them. Furthermore, a prediction market mechanism allows participants to stake on the continued validity or future modification of claims, creating a continuous, financially-incentivized audit of the wiki's content and directly rewarding those who correct errors.
